segments.s612-g305: سپَه را is the definite object (سپَه 'army' + را object marker) placed before the rest for emphasis. ز is the poetic form of از 'from', so ز سالار گردنکشان literally means 'from the chiefs who are neck‑pullers' (i.e. the rebellious chiefs) and functions as the source/agent phrase.
segments.s612-g306: بجستند is past tense, 3rd plural of جستن and here means 'they sought/asked for' (transitive). زان is an anaphoric/adverbial 'from that' or 'thereupon' linking to the previous clause; تازیانه نشان is the direct object phrase 'the whip as a token' with نشان functioning appositionally ('as a sign/token').
segments.s612-g307: سپاه انجمن شد is a predicative structure: سپاه (subject) + انجمن شد ('became an assembly' → 'assembled'). به درگاه is a locative phrase 'at the court/threshold'. The final بر is a linking/prepositional particle that leads into the next locative clause.
segments.s612-g308: کجا is a relative/locative 'where' introducing where they remained; همچنان 'still/remaining' modifies the state. بر در is an idiomatic pair meaning 'at/on the door/gate of', and شاه‌بر here denotes the royal threshold/royal place, so the whole means 'where they remained at the royal gate.'
segments.s612-g309: هرانکس که is a free relative 'whoever (that)'. تازانه here qualifies the person as 'newly arrived'/'the newcomer' and combines with دانست (past of دانستن) 'knew/recognized'. باز is an adverb 'again/back' that anticipates the action in the next clause.
segments.s612-g310: برفتند is past 3rd plural 'they went/returned' (here 'returned'). The clause is coordinated with و بردند 'and they carried/brought'; پیشش is a locative/prepositional adverb 'before him/in his presence', and نماز is the object 'prayer' (they brought prayer before him).
segments.s612-g311: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دربان is the subject 'the doorkeeper' and بدید is the simple past 'saw'. آن سپاه‌گران is the demonstrative-marked object 'those soldiers/warriors' that he saw.
segments.s612-g312: This is a descriptive phrase listing the warriors' appearance: کمردار 'girded/with belts' modifies an implied 'men', بسیار 'many' quantifies them, و links to the next descriptor, ژوپین 'gambeson/padded jacket' and وران is a poetic plural/participle‑like suffix indicating 'those wearing' (i.e. 'gambeson‑wearers'). The whole paints 'many belted and gambeson‑clad ones.'
segments.s612-g89: This clause states the main result: 'جوان را' sets 'the young man' as the topical/affected person (را here is a topical/emphatic marker). 'شود' is the present/subjunctive-like/poetic form of 'شد' meaning 'become'. The phrase 'گوژ بالای راست' is a noun phrase describing the kind and location of the deformity: 'گوژ' (humpbacked) + 'بالای راست' ('on the upper/right side'), with the head adjective before the locative.
segments.s612-g90: 'ز کار زنان' is a causal prepositional phrase ('ز' = from/because of + 'کار' = deeds/affairs + 'زنان' = of women). 'چندگونه' modifies 'بلاست' and means 'many / various kinds of'. 'بلاست' is the predicate asserting that such various calamities exist/are the case; the verb 'است' is contracted into the nominal form 'بلاست' here (poetic).
segments.s612-g91: 'به یک ماه یک‌بار آمیختن' is an adverbial time/frequency phrase: 'به ... یک‌بار' = 'once per/once every ...' so the whole phrase reads 'to mingle once per month' (i.e., 'more than once a month' in context). 'آمیختن' is the verbal noun 'to mingle' (here used for sexual intercourse).
segments.s612-g92: This is a conditional clause: 'گر' = 'if'. 'افزون بود' = 'it was/might be more' (past/imperfect sense); the second 'بود' functions as an auxiliary giving a conditional/past sense to 'ریختن' that follows: 'خون بود ریختن' = 'blood would be shed' or 'there would be bleeding'. Word order is poetic (auxiliary after noun).
segments.s612-g93: 'همین بار' = 'this very burden/occasion' (همین = this same, بار = burden/occasion). 'از بهر' is a fixed poetic prepositional compound meaning 'for the sake of / on account of'. 'فرزند را' marks 'the child' as the beneficiary/topic with را used for emphasis/topic.
segments.s612-g94: 'بباید' is an impersonal/modal verb meaning 'it must / one ought to'. In this structure the logical subject follows: 'جوان خردمند را' — 'the wise young man' (جوان خردمند) with را again topical/emphatic; the clause says that the wise youth must (bear/do) this for the child's sake. The verb's object (what must be done) is understood from the preceding phrase.
segments.s612-g95: 'چو' here functions as a conditional 'if/when'. 'افزون کنی' is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive-like clause 'you increase (it)'; Persian often omits the object when clear from context. 'کاهش افزون کند' states the consequence: 'the decrease/diminution becomes greater' (note the repetition of افزون to stress increasing). Verb 'کند' is a third-person present/past-form used to state the resulting action.
segments.s612-g96: 'ز سستی تن' is a causal phrase 'from the body's weakness' ('ز' = from/because of + 'سستی' = weakness + 'تن' = body). 'مرد بی‌خون کند' is a result clause: 'it makes a man bloodless' — 'کند' is the causative/result verb 'makes/causes', and 'بی‌خون' is the predicate adjective 'without blood/bloodless' applied to 'مرد' (man/person).
segments.s612-g49: This clause begins with بدو, an older prepositional/indirect object form meaning “to him” (not the imperative “run!” here). Word order is topical: indirect object (بدو) + subject (سرشبان) + past verb گفت (“said”), followed by a quoted/command clause introduced by ز ‘from’ plus the locative ایدر “here” and the imperative برو “go.” Together it means “The head‑groom said to him, ‘From here, go!’.”
segments.s612-g50: This second clause continues the head‑groom’s instruction or promise. دهی is a verbal form meaning roughly “you will be given / you shall get” (classical/fixed form used to express outcome), modified by تازه “fresh.” پیش here means “before / in front (of),” and اندر آیدت نو literally “before the new comes to you” (اندر + آیدت = ‘comes upon/into you’). The whole string expresses: “you shall receive something fresh before anything new reaches you.”
segments.s612-g51: This clause uses the locative motion pattern: به شهر آید آواز زان جایگاه = “to the city comes a sound from that place.” The preposition به + noun (شهر) indicates direction/target, آید is the present/habitual form “comes” used narratively, آواز is the thing that comes (“sound/cry”), and زان (زا + ن) is the archaic poetic preposition “from that.”
segments.s612-g52: This short prepositional phrase specifies location: به نزدیکی کاخ بهرامشاه = “at/near the vicinity of the palace of Bahram‑Shah.” به is the standard preposition, نزدیکی is a noun meaning “nearness/close by,” and the following nouns identify the place (کاخ = palace; بهرامشاه = the king Bahram).
segments.s612-g53: چو here is the temporal conjunction “when.” گردون (literary for ‘the sky’ or ‘heaven/fate’) is the subject, and بپوشد is the classical/subjunctive/present form of پوشیدن used in temporal clauses to mean “puts on” or “dons.” حریر سیاه is a noun phrase “black silk” (حریر = silk, سیاه = black) serving as the garment the sky ‘puts on’ — a poetic way to say night falls.
segments.s612-g54: This clause states the resulting action: به جشن آید آن مرد با دستگاه = “that man came to the feast with pomp.” آید (comes) is a narrative present, آن مرد is the demonstrative + noun “that man,” and با دستگاه is an idiomatic phrase meaning “with pomp/regalia” (دستگاه here = courtly apparatus/ceremonial trappings).
segments.s612-g55: گر is the conditional conjunction “if.” ایدونک is a small, colloquial/poetic particle meaning roughly “so/like that” or softening the condition; باشدت is a classical 3rd‑person/optative form meaning “be to you / you may have” (here functioning as ‘should there be to you’), and لختی درنگ is the object of that state: “a short pause / a brief delay.” Together: “If there should be for you a short delay…”
segments.s612-g56: This clause gives the consequence: به گوش آیدت نوش و آواز چنگ = “to the ear will come نوش and the song of the harp.” به گوش آیدت is literally “will come to your ear,” نوش here is a poetic noun meaning “delight / pleasantness” (often used of pleasant drink or pleasure — here auditory delight), و is the conjunction “and,” آواز چنگ = “the song/sound of the harp.”
segments.s612-g121: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause; the clause follows normal older Persian order with the subject شاه ('the king') then the locative اندر آمد ('entered'). چنان جای دید is the main predication of that clause: چنان ('such/so') qualifies جای ('place') and دید is the past tense 'he saw/observed'.
segments.s612-g122: This continues the main clause with an object phrase; the subject (the king) is omitted and carried over. پرستنده هر جای برپای دید literally lists the object seen: 'worshippers (پرستنده) in every place (هر جای) standing/erect (برپای)' followed by the past verb دید ('saw'). برپای functions as a participial/adverbial modifier 'standing'.
segments.s612-g123: چنین گفت is 'thus he said' with چنین as an adverb 'thus' before گفت (here used as a speech verb). کای is the archaic vocative 'O', followed by epithets دادگر ('just') and خدای ('God'); together they form a vocative address ('O just God' / 'O just one, God').
segments.s612-g124: This is an imperative or optative request: به خوبی توی 'to/into the goodness in you' (به + خوبی + توی, where توی = in you), بنده را is the direct object marked by را ('your servant'), and رهنمای is the verb 'guide' (a classical verb-form used here as a plea/imperative). Word order places the goal ('to your goodness') before the object and verb for emphasis.
segments.s612-g125: مبادا is an optative/negative particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'let there not be'; جز از 'except from/of' introduces the limiting phrase. داد آیین من groups as 'the rule/custom of justice of mine' (داد qualifying آیین), with من as the possessive 'my' placed after آیین in classical Persian order.
segments.s612-g126: مباد is another form of the optative 'may it not be'. The list آز و گردنکشی names the things to be excluded ('greed and defiance'), and دین من ('my religion') is the domain in which they should not occur. The conjunction و simply links the two nouns.
segments.s612-g127: همه کار و کردار من is a compound subject 'all my work and deeds/behavior' with the possessive من following the coordinated nouns (classical order). داد باد places the predicate noun داد ('justice') before باد, the optative particle meaning 'may it be', yielding the formulaic wish 'may all my deeds be justice' (i.e., be just).
segments.s612-g128: دل زیردستان is a genitive construction 'the heart(s) of the subordinates/subjects'. به ما is the prepositional phrase 'toward us' (به + ما), شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy', and باد again is the optative 'may', forming the wish 'may the hearts of the subjects be glad toward us.'
segments.s612-g41: نگویی here functions as an archaic 2nd‑person verbal form meaning 'tell' (an imperative/subjunctive sense in older Persian narrative); مرا is the dative/indirect object 'to me'. کاین ددان = 'these beasts' (کاین = 'these/that', ددان = 'beasts/predators') is the direct object phrase. ار که کشت begins a dependent clause 'who/that killed (them)' where ار is an older conditional/relative particle and که introduces the relative clause with کشت 'killed'.
segments.s612-g42: که introduces the clause giving a wish/blessing; او را is the pronoun 'him' with the object/contrast marker را (here marking the beneficiary of the blessing). خدای جهان = 'God of the world' (title). باد is the optative particle meaning 'may (it be)' and پشت means 'back' in the sense 'support, protector' — together 'may the God of the world be his support.'
segments.s612-g43: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' with بدو = 'to him' (dative) preceding گفت ('said'); بهرام is the speaker's name in the sentence; کاین هر دو شیر = 'these both lions' (کاین = 'these/that', هر دو = 'both') specifying the animals referred to.
segments.s612-g44: تبه شد is a compound meaning 'were ruined/killed' (تبه here used in passive/result sense and شد is the past/aspect auxiliary 'became/was'); به + پیکان marks the agent/instrument with به meaning 'by' or 'with' in this context; مرد دلیر = 'a brave/valiant man' is the agent ('the arrow of a brave man'). Word order places the result (تبه شد) before the agent phrase.
segments.s612-g45: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction) introducing the clause. شیران جنگی = 'war‑lions' or 'battle‑lions' (شیران 'lions', جنگی used adjectivally 'warlike' or 'of the war'). بکشت is an archaic/perfective verbal form meaning 'he killed' (the prefix ب- is an old narrative marker attached to the verb stem); او is the subject 'he'. برفت = 'he went off/rode away' (بر‑رفت = departed) — the verb of motion ending the clause.
segments.s612-g46: سواری سرافراز = 'a proud/triumphant rider' (noun + adjectival epithet). با یار هفت = 'with seven companions' where با = 'with', یار = 'companion/ally', and هفت = 'seven' follows the noun (numeral after the counted noun is common in classical style). The whole phrase describes the manner of his departure.
segments.s612-g47: کجا باشد is a rhetorical question 'where is (it)?' with باشد as the subjunctive/optative copula used in questions. ایوان گوهرفروش = 'the jeweler's iwan/portico' (ایوان = porch/arcade, گوهرفروش = jeweler); the noun phrase names the place being asked about.
segments.s612-g48: پدیدار کن is an imperative 'make (it) appear/bring to view' (پدیدار = 'visible/manifest' + کن imperative 'do'). راه = 'way/path' is the direct object; و = 'and' links the second imperative بر ما مپوش = 'do not conceal from us' where بر here means 'from/toward' with ما 'us' and مپوش is negative imperative 'do not hide/cover'. The two imperatives form a request to reveal and not conceal the path.
segments.s612-g289: پرستنده را گفت = 'he said to the attendant/Devotee'. The object (پرستنده) is marked by را before the verb گفت, following a common Persian word order where an explicit object precedes the verb; گفت is the reporting verb introducing an imperative quote that follows. درها ببند is the quoted imperative clause ('close the doors'), with درها (plural) as the direct object and ببند as the 2nd/3rd-person imperative.
segments.s612-g290: یکی را بتاز از پس گوسفند = an imperative ordering someone to ride/charge. یکی را uses را to single out 'one person' as the one to be sent; بتاز is an imperative 'rush/gallop' directed at that person; از پس + noun means 'after' or 'in pursuit of' (literally 'from behind'), so از پس گوسفند = 'after the sheep'.
segments.s612-g291: نباید که آرند خوان بی‌بره = an impersonal negative clause. نباید introduces an impersonal prohibition ('it ought not / must not be'); که introduces the subordinate clause; آرند is an archaic/classical 3rd-person plural form meaning 'they bring' (subjunctive/present) and takes خوان ('banquet/table') as its object; بی‌بره 'without a lamb' modifies خوان.
segments.s612-g292: بره نیز پرورده باید سره = a verbless classical clause with emphatic word order. بره (the lamb) is topic; نیز 'also' links it to the previous idea; پرورده 'reared/nurtured' describes the lamb; باید is the modal 'must/ought' and سره 'pure/choice' qualifies how it should be. Word order (topic — modifiers — modal) is typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s612-g293: چو بیدار گردد فقاع و یخ آر = a temporal subordinate clause. چو 'when' introduces the clause; بیدار is the predicate adjective 'awake' placed before the verb; گردد is a classical subjunctive/optative form of 'to become' meaning 'becomes / is roused'; فقاع و یخ are the compound subject linked by و. (آر here is a lexical item attached to یخ in the line and functions as part of the phrase — its exact nuance is poetic/archaic.)
segments.s612-g294: همی باش پیش گشسپ سوار = main imperative clause. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('then / just / at that moment'); باش is the imperative 'be' (be present); پیش means 'before / in the presence of'; گشسپ سوار is the person addressed in apposition (Goshasp, the rider). The order places the adverb and verb before the locative phrase.
segments.s612-g295: یکی جام کافور بر با گلاب = a noun phrase describing the remedy: 'one cup (یکی جام) of camphor (کافور) with rosewater (با گلاب)'. Classical word order places the measure/quantity before the noun (یکی جام) and uses بر 'on/upon' in an instructive/poetic sense linking کافور to the medium (here 'with' rosewater).
segments.s612-g296: چنان کن که بویا بود جای خواب = an instruction clause headed by the imperative کن ('do so'). چنان کن 'make it so' is followed by a purpose/result clause introduced by که. بویا بود is an archaic/perfect expression meaning 'was fragrant' or 'should be smelling' (بویا is an older form and بود is the past/copular auxiliary); جای خواب 'the sleeping-place' is the target affected by the action.
segments.s612-g25: بدو is the prepositional pronoun (به + او) meaning “to him/unto it” placed before the verb for emphasis. سرشبان is the speaker’s title ‘the head‑groom’ and گفت is the past verb ‘said’; کای is the vocative particle ‘O’ addressing شهریار (‘king’). Word order is topical/emphatic (to‑him + speaker + said + vocative).
segments.s612-g26: This clause expresses motion and origin. ز گیتی = ‘from the world’ (ز as poetic ‘from’ + گیتی ‘world’). من آیم is a first‑person form (آیم) used as ‘I have come / I come’ with من explicit. بدین مرغزار functions as ‘to this meadow’ (بدین = به + این, the preposition ب + demonstrative ‘this’); the given segmentation separates ب and دین but here they combine to mean ‘to this’.
segments.s612-g27: همین gives emphasis ‘these very’. گوسفندان is the plural noun ‘sheep’. گوهرفروش follows as a post‑nominal identifier (here the jeweler’s/the gem‑seller’s), so the phrase reads ‘these very sheep of the jeweler’ (possession expressed by juxtaposition, common in poetic usage).
segments.s612-g28: آوردم is the first‑person past tense ‘I brought’. به دشت اندر آوردم = ‘I brought (them) into the plain’ (به دشت = into the plain; اندر intensifies locative ‘in/into’). از کوه دوش places source and time: از کوه = ‘from the mountain’, دوش = ‘last night’ (temporal adverb modifying آوردم).
segments.s612-g29: توانگر is an address or epithet ‘wealthy’ (here adjectival used as vocative), خداوند is an honorific ‘lord/master’, so توانگر خداوند is a vocative phrase ‘O wealthy lord’ (or ‘wealthy master’). این گوسفند follows as the direct object/topic ‘this sheep’. There is no copula; the sequence is a vocative followed by the referent.
segments.s612-g30: بپیچد is b‑ + پیچد (from پیچیدن) used here in an optative/jussive sense ‘may (he/it) turn/turn away’; همی is the frequentative/continuative adverb ‘always/ever’. از marks the source ‘from’, نهیب means ‘rebuke/cry of warning’, and گزند means ‘harm/damage’; the phrase as a whole expresses a wish that the sheep be kept away from harm caused by reproach.
segments.s612-g31: به خروار is a quantitative prepositional phrase ‘by the kharvar / a great quantity’. با = ‘with’ and نامور modifies گوهرست: نامور گوهرست = ‘(it) is famed/renowned gems’. گوهرست is a nominal/predicative form ‘is gems/precious stones’ (‑ست = copula). The line states quantity + material: ‘a great amount with famed gems’.
segments.s612-g32: همان = ‘that/indeed’, followed by a series of nominal predicates linked by و: زر و سیمست و هم زیورست = ‘(it) is gold and is silver and also is ornament’. The suffix ‑ست on سیمست and زیورست is the copular ending, so each noun is presented as a category of the treasure.
segments.s612-g377: بدو (a prepositional pronoun) + گفت (past of 'say') + شاه is a poetic/inverted word order: verb (گفت) comes before the subject (شاه). بدو = 'to him,' so the phrase means 'the king said to him.' The following clause starting with این کجا را is the reported question ('Where did you have this?').
segments.s612-g378: مرا (م + را) is the accusative 'me' (م marked by object particle را). مست کردی is a two-word predicate: مست 'intoxicated' + کردی (2nd-person singular past 'you did') = 'you made (me) drunk.' و links to the next verb بگذاشتی, where بگذاشتی is 2nd-person past 'you left/let (it be)/abandoned' (here 'left me'); the sequence states two actions done by the addressee.
segments.s612-g379: همان is emphatic 'that/the very', followed by the coordinated noun phrase چامه و چنگ 'poem and harp'. ما را uses ما with the object marker را to mark the beneficiary/experiencer 'for us'; بس است is a verblike copula phrase meaning 'is enough.' The overall clause = 'That poem and harp suffice for us.'
segments.s612-g380: نثار زنان is a nominal phrase 'offerings/dedications to women.' بهر = 'for' or 'intended for' and together with دیگر کس است ('are for another person') states possession/appropriation: 'the dedications for women belong to some other person.' Est is the copula 'is.'
segments.s612-g381: بیار is the imperative 'bring.' آنک گفتی = 'that which you said' (آنک = 'that/which'; گفتی here functions as 'you said' or 'you told'); ز نخچیرگاه 'from the hunting-ground' uses the poetic preposition ز = 'from.' So the clause requests bringing the thing mentioned that came from the hunting-field.
segments.s612-g382: This is a list introduced by ز 'from/of' giving the material: ز رزم 'of battle' و سر نیزه 'and (the) spear's head/tip' و زخم شاه 'and the king's wound.' Note the repeated coordination with و; سر is 'head/tip' as a modifier of نیزه 'spear.'
segments.s612-g383: ازان پس = 'thereupon/after that.' بدو = 'to him.' گفت is again the past 'said' (verb) and گوهرفروش is the address or speaker label 'the jeweler.' The sequence reports that the jeweler then said these words to the addressee.
segments.s612-g384: کجا شد که ... is an interrogative 'where has (someone) gone/what became of (someone) that ...' where شد is perfective/past 'became/was' and که introduces the clause describing the person: ما مست گشتیم دوش = 'we became drunk last night' (ما subject, مست 'drunk', گشتیم 'we became' or 'we were made,' دوش = 'last night'). Together: 'Where is he who (caused) us to be drunk last night?'
segments.s612-g241: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (gaze/look) + کن (do!) = 'look/watch.' بدو here is به + او ('to/at him') used after the verb as its target. تا introduces a temporal/result clause 'until/so that'; پسند آیدت is a fixed expression 'it comes to you as liking' = 'you find it pleasing' (پسند 'liking/approval' + آیدت 'it comes to you', with -ت the 2nd‑person enclitic).
segments.s612-g242: بر آسودگی is a prepositional phrase 'for/on the sake of ease/relief' (بر = 'for/toward', آسودگی = 'rest/ease'). سودمند is an adjective 'useful/profitable' placed before the verb. آیدت is the same verb form as above ('it comes to you' = 'it will prove to you'), with enclitic -ت 'to you'; the whole means 'it will be useful for your ease.'
segments.s612-g243: چنین گفت = 'thus said/spoke.' با here functions in an older/poetic sense as the preposition introducing the addressee ('to') and is followed by the name ماهیار (Mahyar). آرزوی (literally 'the Wish') is the speaking agent placed after the clause (a noun used as the speaker). Note: the lemma entry for گفت in the provided senses lists 'conversation' rather than the verb 'said,' so that sense does not match the use here.
segments.s612-g244: که‌ای is an archaic/questioning 'who are you' (که + second‑person clitic -ای), used to address someone. باب here is a vocative/title (difficult to render by the provided senses; it functions as an address). آزاده and نیک خوی are epithets: آزاده 'noble‑hearted/free' and نیک خوی 'of good disposition' (خوی = disposition). Grammatically this is a vocative/exclamatory question addressed to 'باب.'
segments.s612-g245: مرا گر (مراگر) = 'if it were to me' / 'if you (were to) ... me' (conditional 'if'). همی is the old/emphatic imperfective particle marking continuing action. داد is the verb 'give' (here as the lexical verb), and خواهی is the 2nd‑person auxiliary 'you will/wish' (a classical periphrastic future/intent: verb + خواهی). به کس = 'to anyone' (به + کس). Together: 'If you will give me to anyone...'
segments.s612-g246: همالم is 'my hamāl/companion/carrier' with the possessive -م 'my' built into the word; گشسپ is the proper name Goshasp in apposition; سوارست = سوار + است 'is mounted/horseman' (predicate); و بس = 'and enough/that is all.' The clause asserts the speaker's companion is the mounted hero Goshasp, and that suffices.
segments.s612-g247: تو گویی is a hypothetical/hedging expression 'you might say' or 'you would say' (تو = 'you', گویی = present/subjunctive 'you say' used to mean 'one might say'). به بهرام = 'with/at Bahram' (به used locatively/associative). ماند همی = ماند (remain) + همی (emphatic/continuous particle) 'remains/stays indeed.' The phrase describes the idea that 'one would say he remains with Bahram.'
segments.s612-g248: چو = 'as/for' (giving a reason or comparison); جانست = 'is life' i.e. 'is dear/precious' (an idiom: 'he is like life' = very dear); و با او نشستن = 'and to sit with him' where نشستن is an infinitival/masdar‑like verbal noun 'sitting/to sit'; دمی = 'a moment' (temporal). Grammatically this gives the reason: because he is dear, even sitting with him a moment is significant.
segments.s612-g329: بیامد is a past-tense 3rd-person singular verb 'came' placed before its noun (verb-first order is common in poetry). پرستنده is the subject 'the worshipper/devotee'. هنگام روز is an adverbial phrase 'at the time of day / by day' (هنگام + روز). The whole segment thus means 'a worshipper came by day.'
segments.s612-g330: که introduces a subordinate clause (here temporal/relative: 'when' or 'such that'). پیدا is an adjectival predicate 'visible/apparent' and نبد is the archaic negative past of بودن ('was not' ≈ پیدا نبود). هور گیتی‌فروز is a poetic compound epithet 'the light/sun that lights the world' (هُور + گیتی + فروز). The clause reads 'when the world-illuminating sun was not visible.'
segments.s612-g331: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain / one'. تازیانه is the head noun 'whip'. به زر تافته is a material/agent phrase 'woven/worked with gold' where به marks the material and تافته is a past participle/adjectival 'woven'. Together: 'a whip woven with gold.'
segments.s612-g332: به هرجای is a prepositional phrase 'in/to every place' (به + هرجای = 'wherever / everywhere'). گوهر modifies بافته: گوهر ... بافته = 'woven with gems'; بافته is the past participle 'woven'. The intervening برو functions as a poetic/distributive element (intensifier) signalling 'throughout' or 'all over' rather than its literal verb meaning. The segment thus means 'with gems woven everywhere.'
segments.s612-g333: بیاویخت is a past 3rd-person singular verb 'he hung (it up)' formed with the classical aorist/past. از پیش درگاه ما is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in front of/ before our threshold or court' (از پیش used poetically for 'before/in front of'). Word order (verb before noun phrase) is typical in Ferdowsi's verse.
segments.s612-g334: بدان سو is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto that side' (ب + دان + سو functioning together). که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; باشد is the classical 3rd-person present/subjunctive copula 'is/should be' used here as 'which is'; گذرگاه ما is the noun phrase 'our passage/way'. Entire clause: 'on that side which is our passage.'
segments.s612-g335: ز is the poetic form of از 'from/of' used here to mark source/agent in the temporal clause. دربان is 'the gatekeeper'. چو بشنید introduces a temporal clause 'when he heard' (چو = 'when', بشنید = past 'he heard'). یکسر is an adverb 'at once / immediately' modifying the action and سخن is the object 'speech/words.'
segments.s612-g336: بپیچید is the past 3rd-person singular of پیچیدن with the prefix ب- (here 'he turned' / 'he twisted/turned aside'). The noun phrase 'بیدار مرد کهن' (alert old man) follows the verb; بیدار is an adjective 'awake/alert', مرد 'man', کهن 'old/ancient' — adjectives follow the noun in classical usage but here are arranged for metrical/poetic effect. The full sense: 'the alert old man turned.'
segments.s612-g145: پرستنده را نیز خوان خواستند is an object-following construction: پرستنده (the worshipper) takes the object marker را. نیز 'also' modifies the whole action. خوان خواستند here is a light verbal phrase meaning 'called/asked to the table' (خوان = feast/table, خواستند = they wanted/asked), so the clause means 'they also invited/called the worshipper to the feast.'
segments.s612-g146: یکی جای دیگر بیاراستند: یکی = 'one/a' (indefinite here), جای دیگر = 'another place'. بیاراستند is past plural 'they made ready / they set up / they adorned.' Together it means 'they prepared/set up another place.' The construction is straightforward SOV-like ordering with the verb at the end.
segments.s612-g147: همان میزبان را یکی زیرگاه: همان میزبان را = 'that same host' marked as direct object. یکی زیرگاه = 'a low place/seat' (یکی used as an indefinite numeral 'a/one'). The noun phrase is positioned before the verb نهادند which completes the meaning 'they put/placed that host in a low seat.'
segments.s612-g148: نهادند و بنشست نزدیک شاه: نهادند is past plural 'they placed/set (him)'. The conjunction و links the next clause; بنشست is past (3rd person) 'he sat down', so the subject shifts to the host who then sat. نزدیک شاه is a locative adjunct 'near the king.' The sequence describes two actions in order: they set him down and (he) sat near the king.
segments.s612-g149: به پوزش بیاراست پس میزبان: به پوزش (literally 'with an apology' / 'having apologized') is a circumstantial phrase; بیاراست is used figuratively as 'made/expressed' (here 'made an apology'). پس میزبان = 'then the host' introduces the agent who speaks next. Word order places the circumstantial phrase before the subject and verb.
segments.s612-g150: به بهرام گفت ای گو مرزبان: به بهرام گفت = 'he said to Bahram' (به marks the indirect object). The following sequence ای گو مرزبان is vocative plus an imperative: ای = 'O' (vocative), گو is the imperative 'speak/say,' and مرزبان is the addressee 'marzban' (border‑governor). So the host addresses Bahram with 'O marzban, speak!'.
segments.s612-g151: توی میهمان اندرین خان من: توی...اندرین = 'among/in these' locative framing the clause; میهمان = 'guests'; خان = 'khan/inn' (here a caravanserai). The pronoun من at the end is the nominal predicate 'I' (Persian often places the pronoun after locative phrases), so literally 'I am among the guests in this khan.'
segments.s612-g152: فدای تو بادا تن و جان من: فدای تو بادا is an optative/formulaic expression 'may (I) be sacrificed for you' (فدای = 'sacrifice for', بادا = 'may it be'). تن و جان من = 'my body and my life/soul' with the possessor من placed after the nouns. The whole line expresses devoted subservience: 'may my body and soul be yours.'
segments.s612-g97: برفتند is a past-tense, third-person plural verb 'they went/departed'. گویان is a present-participle/gerund 'saying' used adverbially after the verb to indicate manner (they went while saying). به ایوان شاه is a prepositional phrase marking destination: به + place (ایوان, 'iwan/porch') with شاه ('the king') as the possessor.
segments.s612-g98: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one/someone' (a certain person). گفت is the past verb 'said' (the provided sense list lacks the lexical 'said' sense, so use -1 if forced; here we note it functions as a simple past reporting verb). خورشید گم کرد راه is a short clause: خورشید 'the sun' (subject), گم 'lost' (adjective/participle) plus کرد as the light verb 'made/did' forming the verbal phrase 'lost' (گم کردن), and راه 'the way' as the object.
segments.s612-g99: شب تیره‌گون is a noun phrase 'the dark-hued night' (تیره‌گون adjectival). رفت is the past verb 'went'. In Persian poetry the subject (بهرام گور, the proper name 'Bahram Gūr') is often placed after the verb, so here رفت governs and 'بهرام گور' is the subject/name following it; گور here is the epithet 'Gūr' (onager) as part of the name.
segments.s612-g100: پرستنده is a present-participial/agentive form 'worshipping' or 'one who worships'. یک تن is 'one person' (تن = person/body). ز بهر is a compound prepositional causal/aim phrase ('for the sake of' or 'because of'): ز (archaic/poetic 'from/for/because of') + بهر ('for/toward'). ستور is the target of that phrase 'steed/horse'. The whole phrase modifies or describes someone devoted to a person on account of a steed.
segments.s612-g101: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. آواز چنگ is 'the sound/voice of the harp' (آواز + instrument چنگ). اندر آمد is an archaic phrasing 'came into' (اندر = 'in/within', آمد = 'came'). به گوش literally 'to the ear' (meaning 'reached the ear' or 'was heard').
segments.s612-g102: بشد is an archaic/perfective verb here meaning 'it happened/they rose/became' (past occurrence). شاه تا خان گوهر فروش lists affected people in a range: شاه 'the king' ... تا 'even/up to' (linking a range or extent) ... خان (here a title or person) گوهر فروش 'the gem-seller' (گوهر = gem, فروش = seller). Syntactically this enumerates persons who reacted when the harp was heard.
segments.s612-g103: همی is an older adverbial particle indicating continuous or immediate action ('kept on/then/just'), here giving aspectual coloring before the verb. تاخت is the past 'galloped/rushed'. باره here is an adverbial/temporal noun 'on that occasion/once' (marking the single action). به آواز چنگ is a manner phrase 'to the sound of the harp' (به + noun).
segments.s612-g104: سوی introduces direction 'toward(s)'. خان here most plausibly means an inn/caravanserai (sense 1) when paired with بازارگان 'merchants', so سوی خان بازارگان = 'toward the merchants' khan/inn'. بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'without delay / immediately' qualifying the action.
segments.s612-g137: بدو گفت is a compact phrase meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him' placed before گفت). The quoted wish شب بر تو فرخنده باد uses فرخنده 'auspicious' as the predicate and باد as the optative particle 'may' (classical/poetic). بر تو = 'upon you' (literally 'on you'), so the clause means 'may night be auspicious upon you.'
segments.s612-g138: This is another optative blessing built with باد. همه بدسگالان = 'all the ill‑wishers/ill‑omened people' (subject), ترا (تو + را) = 'to you' / 'for you' where را is the object/definite marker attached to the pronoun, and بنده + باد = 'may (they) be servants'. The clause literally: 'May all ill‑wishers be servants to you.'
segments.s612-g139: نهالی بیفگند و مسند نهاد are two coordinated past actions. نهالی 'a sapling' is the object of بیفگند, an archaic/poetic past form of افکندن meaning 'placed/put/plant(ed)' (here 'planted/put'), و = 'and', مسند نهاد = 'placed/put a seat' (نهاد is past 'put/placed').
segments.s612-g140: ز دیدار او = 'because of/from the sight of him' where ز (poetic) means 'from/on account of'; میزبان گشت شاد = 'the host became glad' with گشت as the past of 'become' and شاد as the predicative adjective 'happy'.
segments.s612-g141: گرانمایه = 'valuable/precious', خوانی here is a nominal use (context: performers or a banquet) rather than the verb senses listed, and بیاورد is a narrative past/remote past 'brought' (classical narration often uses this form). زود is an adverb 'soon/quickly' modifying the action.
segments.s612-g142: برو functions as a narrative continuation ('then/he proceeded') or motion verb 'went' leading into the next action; خوردنیها is the plural 'the edibles/foods' (object), ازان سان = 'in that manner/from that sort' and که بود is a relative/qualifying clause 'as it/they were' (بود = past 'were'). Together: 'then he arranged/served the foods in the same way as they had been.'
segments.s612-g143: بیامد is the past 'came', یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (used here as 'a'), and مرد مهترپرست is a noun phrase 'a man, a sycophant' (مهترپرست an epithet 'sycophant/over‑reverent follower').
segments.s612-g144: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he ordered/said'; تا introduces a subordinate clause with the sense 'so that / that' and here functions like a complementizer; اسپ او را ببست is the subordinate clause where را marks the definite object 'his (own) horse' (او + را) and ببست (subjunctive/optative sense after تا) means 'be tied/let (it) be tied' or 'he should tie'. Overall: 'he ordered that his horse be tied.'
segments.s612-g33: ندارد is third‑person singular present of داشتن ('to have') and here means 'he does not have / he has not'; جز ... از functions together as 'except (for)'; از takes a partitive/source meaning 'from / of' so جز از دختری = 'except for a maiden'. چنگ‌زن follows دختری as a descriptive apposition/compound (poetic word order) meaning 'harp‑player' (a noun used adjectivally).
segments.s612-g34: This is a compact, poetic description: سر جعد زلفش is built as a sequence of modifiers ('head (سر) [of] curl (جعد) — her tress (زلفش)') with the possessive clitic ـش attached to زلف. شکن بر شکن is a repeated noun + preposition pattern (شکن 'breaker' used figuratively) with بر = 'upon', giving the idiom 'breaker upon breaker' → 'curl upon curl'. Word order and repetition are typical of classical Persian poetic style.
segments.s612-g35: نخواهد is third‑person present of خواستن ('to want') here meaning 'he wants/asks not' and together with جز introduces the limit 'wants nothing except...'. از دست دختر is a partitive/source phrase 'from the girl's hand' (دست = 'hand' used literally), and نبید is the object 'wine'. Word order places the exception phrase after the verb in classical style.
segments.s612-g36: کسی ... ندید with the past verb ندید yields 'no one saw' (کسی here functions as 'no one' in a negative clause). مردم پیر = 'the old people / elders' (مردم 'people', پیر 'old/elder' as modifier). ازین سان is a frozen adverbial phrase 'in this manner / like this' (از + این + سان), and the verb ندید comes at the end, the usual Classical Persian order.
segments.s612-g37: اگر introduces a conditional. نیستی is the noun 'nonexistence' (used poetically) so that 'اگر نیستی داد بهرامشاه' reads 'if the existence of Bahram‑shah's justice were absent' → 'if Bahram‑shah's justice did not exist'. Classical poetry often uses nominal forms like نیستی for abstract conditions.
segments.s612-g38: مر is the dative/benefactive 'to/for me'; او را is the object phrase 'him / his' (او + object marker را). کجا is 'where' and ماندی (past-stem used in an irrealis/conditional sense) means 'would remain / be left'. دستگاه ('court, establishment') is the thing that would or would not remain. Word order places the beneficiary (مر) up front for emphasis.
segments.s612-g39: شهنشاه گیتی is 'the sovereign/king of the world' (compound title plus giti 'world'). نکوشد is present negative/negated form of کوشیدن 'to strive/attempt', here 'does not strive / does not seek', and به زر uses به to indicate instrument/means ('by/with gold'). The clause says the ruler does not secure the world through gold.
segments.s612-g40: همان is a demonstrative/adversative 'that very / the same', موبدش is موبد + ـش 'his mobad (Zoroastrian priest)', نیست is the present copula 'is not', and بیدادگر is a predicate meaning 'tyrant / cruel'. The clause denies that his priest is a tyrant.
segments.s612-g321: بدو گفت کاین را چه گویی همی = 'He said to him, "What is this you are saying?"' 'بدو' is the prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him'. 'گفت' is the past verb 'said' (speech-act) and introduces the reported utterance. 'کاین را' places 'this/that' as a definite object (را marks it), and 'چه گویی همی' is a present/progressive question: 'چه' = 'what', 'گویی' is the 2nd-person present/imperative-like form 'you say' (archaic/poetic), and 'همی' is the classical continuous/progressive particle (roughly 'are ...ing' / 'still'), so the clause literally reads 'what you are saying'.
segments.s612-g322: پی شهریاران چه جویی همی = 'What are you seeking after the kings?' 'پی' here means 'after/in pursuit of' and governs the following noun. 'شهریاران' is the plural 'kings/rulers' as the object of the pursuit. 'چه جویی' = 'what do you seek' (چه = what; جویی = search/seek), and 'همی' again marks progressive aspect. Word order places the adverbial/prefix 'پی' before the noun it relates to.
segments.s612-g323: همان چو ز گوینده بشنید مست = 'As soon as (he) heard that from the speaker, (he was) intoxicated.' 'همان چو' is a temporal formula 'just as / as soon as'. 'ز' = 'from' (poetic) marking source 'گوینده' (the speaker). 'بشنید' is the past 'he heard'. 'مست' is a predicative adjective 'intoxicated' (often figurative: enraptured or overjoyed) describing the subject; the subject is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s612-g324: خروشان ازانجای برپای جست = 'with a roar he from that place sprang to his feet.' 'خروشان' is an adverbial/participle meaning 'roaring/tumultuously'. 'ازانجای' = 'from that place/thereupon'. 'برپای جست' literally 'he leapt onto his feet' (برپای = onto his feet/standing; جست = jumped/sprang). The verb 'جست' at the end completes the action begun in the previous clause.
segments.s612-g325: ز دربان برآشفت و گفت این سخن = 'The gatekeeper sprang up and said these words.' 'ز' here indicates source/agent ('from the gatekeeper' or 'the gatekeeper, at once,' poetic usage). 'دربان' is the gatekeeper. 'برآشفت' is the past verb 'became angry / flew into a rage'. 'و' links to the next verb 'گفت' ('and said'), and 'این سخن' ('these words' / 'this speech') is the object of گفت.
segments.s612-g326: نگوید خردمند مرد کهن = 'A wise, old man would not say (this).' Inverted poetic order places the verb-like form 'نگوید' (negative present/gnomic sense 'does not/would not say') before the noun phrase 'خردمند مرد کهن' ('wise, ancient/old man') which functions as the grammatical subject. The clause expresses a general moralizing assertion.
segments.s612-g327: پرستنده گفت ای جهاندیده مرد = 'The worshipper/devotee said, "O worldly‑wise man,"' 'پرستنده' is the speaker's designation (worshipper/adorer). 'گفت' introduces the direct speech. 'ای' is the vocative particle 'O', and 'جهاندیده مرد' is the vocative phrase 'worldly‑wise man' addressed directly.
segments.s612-g328: ترا بر زمین شاه ایران که کرد = 'what did the king of Iran do to you on the earth?' 'ترا' is the accusative 'to you' (here shown as the enclitic parts 'ت' + 'را'); 'بر زمین' is a locative phrase 'on the earth / upon the land' (here: 'on earth' / 'in this land'); 'شاه ایران' is the subject 'the king of Iran'; and 'که کرد' places the verb 'کرد' ('did/made') at the end — the relative/interrogative 'که' here introduces the action being asked about ('what ... did'). The overall order is typical of older/poetic Persian with the verb final.
segments.s612-g409: This short clause is a compact, poetic clause: subject (زمین ‘the earth’) + noun bousé (بوسه ‘kiss’) + past verb داد (‘gave’). آن زمان (‘at that time’) follows as a time adverbial; ماهیار (a name/epithet) is then appended to indicate who immediately acts next. Word order is freer than prose — the verb داد is placed after the object bousه for poetic effect. داد is simple past ‘gave’.
segments.s612-g410: بیاورد is a past-subjunctive/past simple-form used here narratively as ‘brought’ (‘he brought’); خوان is the direct object ‘banquet/spread’. و links the two coordinated predicates. برآراست is a past-tense, poetic variant meaning ‘arranged/adorned’ (here ‘set in order’), and کار is its object (‘affair, business’). The phrase therefore describes the action Māhyār takes after the time-marker in the previous segment.
segments.s612-g411: بزرگان ‘the great ones’ is the subject; که introduces a relative clause ‘who…’; بودند is the past plural copula ‘were’. بر در is an idiomatic prepositional phrase ‘at the door/gate’ (بر used with در); به پای here marks position ‘at the foot / standing at’ (literal ‘to the foot’). The clause as a whole = ‘the great men who were standing at the gate’. Word order follows Persian relative-clause patterns with the relative pronoun که + verb.
segments.s612-g412: بیاوردشان is the verb plus object clitic ‘-شان’ = ‘brought them’; the verb is past tense ‘brought’. مرد is the agent ‘a man’ and پاکیزه‌رای is a compound epithet ‘pure-minded / of pure disposition’ modifying مرد. The usual Persian order is verb (with clitic object) then the agent and its epithet; here the speaker emphasizes the bringing action before identifying the man.
segments.s612-g413: سوی حجره خویش رفت = ‘he went toward his own room’: سوی (toward) + حجره (room) + خویش (his own; here split as خوی + ش in the gloss) + رفت (past ‘went’). آرزوی immediately follows and can be read as a noun ‘his wish/desire’ or a proper name (poetically ambiguous); syntactically it interrupts the line and is linked to the following clause as its topic or instrument.
segments.s612-g414: ز مهمان بیگانه پرچین بروی is elliptical/poetic. ز = ‘because of / from’; مهمان بیگانه = ‘the stranger guest’; پرچین = ‘a hedge/screen’ and بروی here functions idiomatically as ‘before/on (his) face’ or ‘set up before’ (an archaic fused construction). The whole segment means roughly ‘(and) he put up a screen before his face because of the strange guest.’ Because this is a compressed, somewhat idiomatic line, some morphemes are fused or archaic.
segments.s612-g415: همی بود تا = ‘it was still / it continued until’: همی is an emphatic/durative adverb (‘still/kept on’), بود is past ‘was’, تا introduces the temporal limit ‘until’. چرخ (the sky/heaven) is the subject of پوشد, which is subjunctive/present-past poetic form meaning ‘covers/dons’ (here ‘donned darkness’). سیاه follows as the complement ‘black/darkness.’ The clause expresses a continuing state up to a change in the sky.
segments.s612-g416: ستاره پدید آید = ‘a star appears’ (ستاره subject, پدید ‘appear’ participial/verb-stem + آید ‘comes/happens’ as a present/future narrative form). از گرد ماه = ‘from around the moon’: از ‘from’, گرد ‘around/about’, ماه ‘the moon’. The verb phrase is narrative-present/past used in epic poetry to indicate a sudden emergence.
segments.s612-g153: بدو is the prepositional/enclitic phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here following بدو) and introduces speech; بهرام is the speaker's name in the nominative position; تیره شبان is a vocative phrase 'dark shepherd' (تیره modifying شبان). The whole segment literally: 'to him said Bahram, (O) dark shepherd.'
segments.s612-g154: که begins a relative/interrogative clause 'who/that'; یابد is the third-person present/subjunctive form of یافتن used here as 'find/would find'; چنین is a demonstrative/adverb 'such'; تازه‌رو is a compound adjective/noun 'fresh-faced, newcomer' modifying میزبان; میزبان is the noun 'host'. Together: 'who would find such a fresh-faced host?'
segments.s612-g155: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; نان خورده شد is a perfect/passive-type clause 'the bread having been eaten' (خورده 'eaten' with شد as auxiliary); جام باید گرفت is an impersonal construction with باید 'it is necessary/must' plus گرفت 'to take' — 'the cup must be taken' (i.e., one must take the cup).
segments.s612-g156: به خواب uses به + noun to indicate motion/entry 'into sleep'; خوش آرام are two attributive adjectives 'pleasant' and 'calm/peaceful' describing خواب; باید گرفت repeats the impersonal modal 'one must take/enter' with گرفت used idiomatically as 'enter/fall into' (sleep).
segments.s612-g157: به یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'toward/to the gods'; نباید بود is an impersonal negative modal 'one should not be' (نباید = 'should not/must not', بود = 'be'); ناسپاس is the predicate adjective 'ungrateful'. The clause warns 'one should not be ungrateful to the gods.'
segments.s612-g158: دل ناسپاسان places دل 'heart' with ناسپاسان (plural/collective adjectival form of 'ungrateful') as its specifier 'the hearts of the ungrateful'; بود is the past copula 'were/was'; پرهراس is a predicative adjective 'fearful/filled with dread.' The whole means 'the hearts of the ungrateful were fearful.'
segments.s612-g159: کنیزک is the diminutive noun 'little maidservant'; ببرد (here) is a narrative/past-tense form 'took/carried' (برد used with prefix ب-); آبه دستان is a poetic expression 'water in (her) hands' (آبه = آب + emphatic vowel, دستان 'hands'); و تشت adds 'and (a) basin.' The segment lists the maid's actions/objects carried.
segments.s612-g160: ز دیدار مهمان is a prepositional phrase 'at/from the sight of the guest' (ز = از, poetic); همی is the archaic/imperfective particle marking continuous or habitual action ('was/kept'); خیره گشت is a two-word predicate 'became fixed/stared' (خیره 'staring/astonished', گشت = 'became'). Together: 'at the sight of the guest she became staring/was transfixed.'
segments.s612-g81: نبیند is the negative-present/indefinite form (ن + بیند) meaning 'is not seen' / 'no one sees'; چنو (an archaic/spellings-variant of چنان) means 'such' or 'so'; کس is the indefinite 'person/one'. The prepositional phrase به بالای و زور uses به to link the qualities ('in regard to') and the two nouns بالای (height, highness — poetic form of بالا) and زور (strength, might). Together: 'no one of such height and might is seen.'
segments.s612-g82: به یک تیر = instrumental 'with/by a single arrow' (به here = 'with'); بر هم بدوزد uses بدوزد (present/subjunctive/poetic form of دوختن) meaning 'to sew/stitch' used metaphorically, and بر هم = 'upon/together' so بر هم بدوزد = 'stitches together' or 'binds together'. دو گور = 'two graves'. The whole clause: 'who with one arrow stitches together two graves.'
segments.s612-g83: تبه گردد = 'becomes ruined/ill' (تبه here = 'ruin/mischief' and گردد = 'becomes'). از introduces the cause 'from/because of'; خفت و خیز is a paired nominal phrase meaning 'lying-down and rising' or more broadly 'comings-and-goings, furtive movements' (poetic collocation referring to women's restless movements or attempts); زنان = 'women'. So: 'it is ruined by the (coming-and-going) of women.'
segments.s612-g84: به زودی شود = 'becomes soon / shortly becomes' (به زودی = 'soon/quickly', شود = 'becomes'); سست = 'weak/unsteady'; چون پرنیان = 'like/as silk' (پرنیان = silken, a simile). Entire segment: 'soon becomes weak like silk.'
segments.s612-g85: کند here is the causative verb 'makes/causes' (کند + object); دیده = 'eye'; تاریک = 'dark'; و links the two effects; رخساره = 'face/complexion' (poetic noun) and زرد = 'pale/wan' — so: 'it makes the eye dark and the complexion pale.'
segments.s612-g86: به تن سست گردد = 'the body becomes weak' (به + تن marks the affected locus, سست = 'weak', گردد = 'becomes'); به لب لاژورد = 'at/to the lip (the color) of lapis-lazuli' — poetic image meaning the lips turn a blue/azure like lazhvard (lapis).
segments.s612-g87: ز (poetic preposition) = 'from/because of'; بوی = 'scent/smell'; زنان = 'women'; موی = 'hair'; گردد = 'becomes'; سپید here refers to hair turning white/grey. So: 'Because of the scent of women the hair becomes white.'
segments.s612-g88: سپیدی = 'whiteness' (the state/result just described) and کند = 'causes/makes'; در جهان = 'in the world'; ناامید = 'hopeless' — the clause states that that whiteness brings hopelessness/status of despair in the world: 'the whiteness makes (him) hopeless in the world.'
segments.s612-g193: شود is an optative/impersonal verb meaning 'may it happen' or 'let it be'; it introduces an expressed wish. ماهیار is the subject (the name/epithet) placed after the verb in poetic order. ایدر امشب are local and temporal adverbs ('here, tonight') inserted before the object. جوان is the direct object 'a young man' (noun appears after the adverbs in this poetic word order).
segments.s612-g194: گروگان کند is a verbal phrase: گروگان 'a hostage' plus کند (do/make) forming 'take/make a hostage' (i.e. 'seize as hostage'). پیش مهمان is a prepositional phrase 'in front of / before the guests' (پیش = before/in the presence of; مهمان = guest). روان here modifies the motion or manner ('moving/going' or 'forth'), qualifying the action of going on or presenting before the guests.
segments.s612-g195: زن چنگ‌زن is an appositive/compound: 'the woman, the harp-player' (زن = woman; چنگ‌زن = harpist). چنگ در بر گرفت is a fixed phrasal idea: چنگ 'the harp' + در بر گرفت 'took into her arms' (در + بر together form the locative/embracing sense); گرفت is simple past 'took/seized'.
segments.s612-g196: نخستین is an adjective 'first' modifying خروش 'cry/roar'. مغان is the plural noun 'the Magians' (the priests/wise men). درگرفت is a compound verb 'burst into / broke out' (perfective past sense), so the clause means 'the Magians burst into their first cry.'
segments.s612-g197: دگر here means 'another/again' or 'then again', introducing a new stanza/turn. چامه را uses the object/topic marker را after 'the poem' ('the poem' is definite/emphasized). باب is 'chapter/section/topic' and functions here as 'stanza/section about...'; خود before ماهیار is emphatic ('the very Mahyār' / 'Mahyār himself'). The whole nominal phrase points to 'another stanza of the poem about Mahyār himself.'
segments.s612-g198: تو گفتی is an address 'you said' (تو = you; گفتی = past 'you said' — here introducing reported speech). بنالد همی is a present/habitual imperfective notion 'keeps moaning/laments' (بنالد with همی conveys continued lamentation). چنگ زار is a noun + postpositive adjective/epithet 'the plaintive (wailing) harp' (زار = plaintive/wailing). The clause reports: 'you said the plaintive harp keeps lamenting.'
segments.s612-g199: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. رود here is the musical sense 'string/instrument' (poetic use), and بریشم is an attributive/possessive-like element (a fine silk/cloth, here poetically naming the string or material) — together they form the instrumental phrase 'my (silken) string/instrument'. سخن‌گوی گشت is 'became a speaker' i.e. 'began to speak/sing' (سخن‌گو = speaker/eloquent; گشت = became).
segments.s612-g200: همه خانه وی is a possessive phrase 'his whole house' (همه = whole/all; خانه = house; وی = his/her/their — here 'his'). سمن بوی گشت is a predicate: سمن 'jasmine' + بوی 'scent' + گشت 'became' — literally 'became the scent of jasmine' i.e. 'was filled with the smell of jasmine.' Word order is poetically inverted but follows noun + predicate structure.
segments.s612-g73: This is a poetic list of courtly items. 'ابا' is the cloak (here used as a noun, not the verbal sense listed), followed by 'یاره' (attendants/companions), then 'تاج' (crown). The preposition 'با' appears only before 'تخت زر' (a golden throne), a typical elision in verse: the repeated 'with' is omitted for the earlier nouns but understood, so the whole phrase means “with cloaks, attendants, crowns, and a golden throne.”
segments.s612-g74: 'درفشان' here is adjectival/participial, 'shining' or 'glittering' (not the literal standard‑bearer sense). 'ز' = 'from/of', marking source or material. 'دیبای رومی' = 'Roman (Byzantine) brocade/silk' (compound noun), and 'گهر' = 'gem(s)'. Together: 'shining with gems from Roman brocade.'
segments.s612-g75: 'شمردست' is a compound adjective meaning 'skillful at counting' or 'nimble‑handed' and modifies 'خادم' (servant/attendant). The preposition 'به' marks the person to whom he is attached: 'to/for the musk‑scented king' ('مشکوی' = musk‑scented). Word order (adj + noun + به + possessor) is typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s612-g76: The relative/prepositional phrase 'کز ایشان' means 'from/of them' (here 'of those [items]'). 'یکی نیست' = 'there is not one' (negative existential). 'بی‌دستگاه' is an adjectival compound meaning 'without ornament/without contrivance' (here negated by 'نیست', so 'not one is without ornament' → all are ornamented). The clause therefore explains that none of the listed things is plain.
segments.s612-g77: 'همی' is an emphatic/frequentative adverb ('keeps on/always'), followed by 'باژ' (a loud cry/crying sound). 'خواهد' here functions as a future/continuous auxiliary ('will/keeps on'), and 'ز هر مرز و بوم' means 'from every border and country' (ز = from; مرز و بوم = frontier and homeland). The phrase says the cry is heard across all lands.
segments.s612-g78: 'به سالی' is an adverbial temporal phrase 'each year' (سالی = yearly). 'پریشان' qualifies the following noun as 'distraught/confused.' Here 'رود' is the verb 'goes' (classical Persian), and 'باژ روم' = 'the cry of Rum' (Rûm = Byzantium/Rome). Literally: 'each year the Roman cry goes forth distraught.' Note: the supplied lemma list for 'رود' gives only noun senses; in context it is a verb, so the sense index is not available from that list.
segments.s612-g79: The interjection 'دریغ' ('alas/pity') begins the exclamation. 'آن' = 'that/the', then a loose list of body parts follows: 'بر' (here meaning brow/forehead — a poetic noun form), 'کتف' (shoulder), and 'بالای شاه' ('the high/upper part of the king' i.e. his elevated head or crest). The repetition of 'دریغ' and the list are rhetorical, lamenting the loss or misfortune of these royal features.
segments.s612-g80: The formula repeats 'دریغ آن' ('alas for that'), followed by 'رخ' (face) and 'مجلس آرای شاه' — a genitive/noun phrase where 'مجلس' = court/assembly and 'آرای' = 'ornaments/adornments' (here 'that which adorns the assembly'). So: 'Alas for that face which adorns the king's court.' The order is noun (face) + modifying genitive phrase, typical in Persian.
segments.s612-g217: نگه + کرد is a compound where نگه (a noun 'gaze') takes the light-verb کرد ('to do') to mean 'to look'. The modal باید ('must') follows the verb in this poetic inversion (نگه کرد باید) and makes the clause jussive: 'one must look'. به روی تو بس places the preposition + noun phrase 'on/at your face' with بس ('enough') qualifying sufficiency: 'it is enough to gaze at your face.'
segments.s612-g218: جز introduces an exception ('except'), او را is marked by the object/topic marker را for emphasis. نمانی is the negative-form of ماندن (with the negative prefix ن-) used jussively/optatively here ('may/shall not remain' or 'let there not remain'), so the clause reads roughly 'let there remain no one.' ز لشکر is a genitive/prepositional phrase 'from the host/army', and به کس ('to/for anyone') narrows the scope: altogether, 'let no one of the army remain except him.'
segments.s612-g219: میانت is میان + enclitic ـت 'your middle/center' (possession expressed by the suffix). چو is the simile particle 'like/as', and غروست is غرو + ـست (copula) meaning 'is (a) sapling/young shoot' — so 'your middle is like a sapling'. بالا چو سرو parallels that: 'and the upper part like a cypress.'
segments.s612-g220: خرامان (an adjective/adverb meaning 'with a proud or stately gait') + شده (perfective/passive auxiliary 'has become') yields 'has become stately'. سرو is the cypress (the thing described), همچون introduces another simile ('like'), and تذرو is an archaic/poetic simile-term; the whole phrase means 'the cypress has become stately, like تذرو.'
segments.s612-g221: به دل = 'in (his) heart' and نره شیر = 'a male lion' (نره qualifying شیر). The coordinating و links to به تن 'in (his) body' + ژنده پیل 'a ragged/old elephant' (ژنده modifying پیل). This is a compact metaphor: 'lion at heart, elephant in the body.'
segments.s612-g222: بناورد is an archaic verb meaning 'brought/caused/constructed/possessed' (here narratively 'he set up' or 'he made'). خشت افگنی is a compound: خشت 'mudbrick' + افگنی (an agentive/causative-form, 'laying/placing' or 'causing') — 'brick-laying' or 'brickwork'. بر دو میل uses بر 'upon' + میل in the sense 'pillar/mast' (archaic architectural sense): 'upon two pillars.'
segments.s612-g223: رخانت derives from رخان (softness/supple complexion) and functions as a nominal/ adjectival quality 'her softness/complexion'. به گلنار ماند درست: به + گلنار ('like/to a pomegranate-blossom'), ماند 'remained' (past), and درست 'exactly/truly' — 'her softness remained exactly like a pomegranate blossom.'
segments.s612-g224: تو گویی here is the idiom 'تو گویی' = 'it is as if / one would say' where گویی is a subjunctive/hypothetical verbal form ('one might say' / 'it seems'). (Note: the provided sense list for گویی does not include this verbal/hypothetical sense, so that item is flagged below.) به می‌برگ گل را بشست: به + می‌برگ ('with a vine-leaf'), گل را (definite object marked by را), بشست 'washed' — 'as if one washed the flower with a vine-leaf.'
segments.s612-g297: من is the 1st‑person subject 'I'. از جام is a prepositional phrase 'with/regarding the cup' (classical use of از to mark the domain/topic). می‌همچنانم is a present/habitual verbal form (mi‑ prefix + همچنانم) meaning 'I remain unchanged / I continue the same'; که introduces the subordinate clause that follows; دوش is an adverb meaning 'last night' and begins that subordinate temporal clause.
segments.s612-g298: نتابد is a 3rd‑person singular present negative of تابیدن here meaning 'does not endure / does not hold'. می‌این functions as a present tense relative/verb 'that comes / who comes' modifying the following noun phrase; پیر گوهر فروش is a compound noun phrase 'old gem/jewel‑seller' (پیر 'old', گوهر 'gem', فروش 'seller'). Together the clause reads roughly 'the coming of that old gem‑seller does not endure' (نتابد taking the coming/arrival expressed by می‌این as its complement).
segments.s612-g299: بگفت is the simple past/preterite 'he/she said' (classical prefixed past). این refers back to the just‑spoken words ('this' / 'the foregoing'). و is the coordinating 'and'. چادر به سر برکشید is a verb phrase: چادر 'veil', به سر 'on the head', برکشید (past) 'drew up / pulled over' — together 'drew the veil over her head'.
segments.s612-g300: تن‌آسانی is a noun meaning 'bodily ease / relaxation' (here indicating she relaxed her body). و 'and' links the next action. خواب در بر کشید is an idiomatic use of کشیدن with در بر 'to draw in/around' meaning 'wrapped herself in sleep' or simply 'fell asleep' (خواب = sleep; در بر کشید = drew/put around herself).
segments.s612-g301: چو here means 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun', تابنده is a post‑nominal adjective 'shining / radiant'. بفراخت is a past form (prefixed ب‑) meaning 'raised / roused / caused to rise' and takes تاج ('crown') as its object — 'when the radiant sun raised the crown'.
segments.s612-g302: زمین شد is the simple past 'the earth became' (شد = become). به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the likeness/manner of' (به + کردار = 'like / as'), and دریای عاج is a genitive phrase 'a sea of ivory' (دریای = 'sea of', عاج = 'ivory'). So 'the earth became like a sea of ivory.'
segments.s612-g303: پرستنده is an active‑participle noun 'worshipper / admirer / devoted follower'. تازانه is an adverb 'newly / freshly' modifying پرستنده. شهریار follows as a post‑nominal possessor ('of the king' / 'the king's') so the phrase reads 'the newly‑devoted admirer of the king.'
segments.s612-g304: بیاویخت is the simple past 3rd‑person 'he/she/it hung on / clung' (bi‑ prefix is an older verbal form). از خانه is a prepositional phrase 'from the house (of...)' indicating the place/source, and ماهیار is a proper name (Mahyār) modifying خانه; together: 'hung from Mahyār's house.'
segments.s612-g345: Sequence of imperatives: کنون ('now') sets the temporal frame, خیز is an imperative verb 'rise!' addressed to the listener, and the coordinating و links it to the following imperative دیبای چینی بپوش ('put on Chinese brocade'). بپوش is the present‑imperative form of پوشیدن ('to wear/put on') and takes دیبای چینی as its object; word order is adverb/temporal → verb → conjunction → object‑verb phrase.
segments.s612-g346: This clause issues another command. بنه here functions as a noun 'a small bundle/heap' to be set (implied verb 'put') rather than the botanical sense; بر سر is the prepositional phrase 'upon the head/of the' and افسر is 'helmet'. چنان هم که دوش is a simile clause ('just as that was last night'), with که introducing the comparison and دوش meaning 'last night'.
segments.s612-g347: نثارش کن is an imperative construction: نثارش ('an offering to him') + کن (imperative 'do/make'). از گوهر شاهوار is a prepositional phrase 'from/of regal gem(s)', where از introduces the source/ material and گوهر شاهوار is the noun phrase describing the kind of jewels.
segments.s612-g348: This gives the specific gift: سه یاقوت سرخ 'three red rubies'. The following از در شهریار uses از + در as a compound meaning 'from the court/door of the king' (در here is used metonymically for the royal court/threshold), شهریار = 'the king'. Word order is numeral → noun → adjective, then a genitive source phrase.
segments.s612-g349: چو بینی is a temporal/subordinate clause head: چو 'when/whenver' + بینی (2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive 'you see' or 'you behold'); رخ شاه خورشیدفش is the noun phrase 'the face of the king, the sun‑bright (epithet)'. The epithet خورشیدفش follows شاه and is appositive.
segments.s612-g350: This is a string of imperatives/instructions. دو تایی ('twofold' / 'two steps') modifies the following برو ('go/advance' — imperative). دست کرده is a participial phrase literally 'having made/prepared the hands' (here 'with hands folded or prepared'), and بکش is an imperative of کشیدن 'draw/pull' used here in the sense 'draw near/bring (yourself) forward' or 'pull (your hands) in'; overall the sequence orders a two‑fold advance with hands prepared and a drawing‑forward action. Word order places the quantifier/adverb before the verb sequence.
segments.s612-g351: مبین here functions as an imperative/optative form related to 'show'/'reveal' (classical usage), followed by the object pronoun مر ('me') and the indirect object ورا ('to him'). چشم در پیش دار is an imperative phrase 'keep your eyes before (i.e., directed downward/in front of) him'; دار at the end is the verb 'hold/keep' (here the available listed senses are nominal and do not capture the verbal use, so the appropriate verbal sense is not among the provided noun senses). The clause orders restraint of the gaze and preservation of posture.
segments.s612-g352: This is a simile/comparative instruction: ورا 'to him', چون 'as/like', روان 'soul/spirit', و 'and', تن 'body', خویش + ش 'one's own' (here split in the tokenization as خوی + ش) form 'one's own', and the final دار is again the imperative 'keep/hold' (verbal use not listed among the provided noun senses). The clause tells the addressee to regard/treat him as if he were the addressee's own soul and body.
segments.s612-g369: چو is an archaic/poetic temporal particle meaning "when" that introduces a subordinate clause. The clause follows a compact SOV/VS poetic order: از (from) marks source, کهتران is the source phrase, آگهی is the noun "news/information," یافت is the past verb "found/learned," and شاه (the subject) appears after the verb by inversion common in verse.
segments.s612-g370: بفرمودشان is the past honorific verb بفرمود plus the object clitic -شان "them," meaning "he ordered them." The following infinitive بازگشتن functions as the verbal complement (the action ordered), and به راه is a prepositional phrase marking destination "to/into the road." Word order is Verb+object-clitic + complement + prepositional phrase.
segments.s612-g371: بفرمود تا is a construction where بفرمود (honorific past "he commanded/said") plus تا introduces the subordinate clause expressing what was ordered (similar to English "ordered that ..." or "commanded so that ..."). رفت is the past tense verb "went," and پیش آرزوی is a prepositional phrase "before/in the presence of Arzū." The clause thus reports the commanded movement.
segments.s612-g372: همی is an archaic adverb meaning "still/always" that gives a durative sense. بودش is the past of بودن with the clinching enclitic -ش ("he/it was"), so together "he was always..." از introduces the source/object of the feeling, and the doubled آرزوی آرزوی is a poetic reduplication for emphasis (intense longing). The whole segment attributes a continuous state of desire toward Arzū.
segments.s612-g373: برفت is the classical past of رفتن (here meaning "went/left"), used in narrative past. با + noun repeats to list accompanying items: با می‌و "with wine/fruit" and با نثار "with offerings/gifts." This segment lists what accompanied Arzū as she departed; the verb precedes its complements as typical in epic diction.
segments.s612-g374: پرستنده is a noun meaning "worshipper/devotee" (here likely an appositive or separate character described in the scene). The repeated با ... و با ... construction coordinates possessions or adornments: با تاج "with a crown" و با گوشوار "and with earrings." The structure lists attributes/items with the repeated preposition for poetic balance.
segments.s612-g375: دو تا گشت is an idiomatic/poetic phrase: دو تا (both/two) + گشت (past of گشتن "became/turned") meaning roughly "they were two" or "the two came together/formed a pair." اندر زمین is a locative prepositional phrase "on the ground," and بوس داد is a light-verb construction where the noun بوس "kiss" is combined with داد "gave," i.e. "gave a kiss" = "kissed." The segment narrates an intimate action on the ground.
segments.s612-g376: بخندید is the simple past "laughed." زو is a poetic/prepositional form (ز + ـو) meaning "at him/from him," marking the target of the laugh. The following و شاه places "the king" in apposition or as the agent associated with the action (word order is flexible in verse). برگشت is another past verb "returned," and شاد is a predicate adjective "happy," giving the result of his return.
segments.s612-g361: 'هم‌انگه' is an adverb meaning 'at that moment' placed before the clause; 'یکی بنده' is an indefinite subject 'a certain servant' (classical 'یکی' = 'one/a certain'); 'آمد' is the past simple 'came' and 'دوان' is an adverb/participle meaning 'running/rushing' that modifies آمد.
segments.s612-g362: The particle 'که' introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'who/that'; 'بیدار شد' is the simple past 'became awake' (i.e. 'awoke'); 'شاه روشن‌روان' is the noun phrase 'the king, the radiant/clear‑souled' with روشن‌روان an epithet. The clause therefore modifies the servant's action ('who woke the king').
segments.s612-g363: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'; followed by 'بیدار شد' ('he awoke/became awake'). 'ایمن و تن‌درست' are predicative adjectives ('safe and unharmed/healthy') joined by 'و', describing the (omitted) subject.
segments.s612-g364: 'به باغ اندر آمد' is archaic word order meaning 'entered into the garden' ('به...اندر آمد' = 'came into'); 'سر و تن بشست' is a coordinated object 'head and body' with the past verb 'بشست' ('he washed') whose subject is omitted but continues to be the same person. Note classical locative 'اندر' (= 'in/into').
segments.s612-g365: 'نیایش کنان' is an adverbial participle 'praying' (کنان = 'doing / while doing') modifying the verb that follows; 'پیش' here means 'in the presence of/before' and 'خورشید شد' = 'he came before the Sun' (poetically 'the Sun' refers to the sovereign or the luminous presence). 'شد' is the simple past verb 'came/appeared'.
segments.s612-g366: The first 'ز' marks agency or source ('by/from God') and 'یزدان' = 'God'; 'دلی پر ز امید' is a nominal phrase 'a heart full of hope' (پر = 'full (of)') and the final 'شد' is the copular/past 'became'. Word order 'پر ز امید شد' = 'became full of hope.'
segments.s612-g367: 'وزانجا' = 'and/from there' (compound classical adverb); 'بیامد' is past 'he came'; 'به جای نشست' = 'sat in his place/took his seat' with 'به' marking direction/goal and 'نشست' the past of 'sit', here 'sat down'.
segments.s612-g368: 'یکی' introduces an indefinite 'a certain (one)'; 'جام می‌خواست' is 'wanted a cup' where 'می‌خواست' is a past/desiderative form 'he wanted/asked for'; 'از می‌پرست' functions as a descriptive/genitive phrase 'a worshipper of wine' (می‌پرست = 'one who worships wine' used adjectivally), so the line reads 'a certain wine‑worshipper wanted a cup.'
segments.s612-g185: به بهرام گفت is an indirect object phrase + verb: به + proper name marks the addressee ('to Bahram'), followed by گفت (past of گفتن, 'said') with the speaker omitted. ای گزیده سوار is a direct vocative: ای (O!) + گزیده (chosen/elite) modifies سوار (rider/horseman). The vocative is quoted or reported by the preceding گفت.
segments.s612-g186: به هر چیز ماننده شهریار is a simile/adverbial phrase: به (in/regard to) + هر چیز (every thing) + ماننده (like/similar to, archaic variant of مانند) + شهریار (king). Word order is typical old/poetic Persian: the comparative/likeness word (ماننده) follows the item it compares.
segments.s612-g187: چنان دان is an imperative/half‑idiom: چنان ('thus/so') + دان (imperative of دانستن / 'consider/know') = 'consider it so' or 'take it thus.' که introduces a clause that explains what to consider. این خانه بر سور تست contains the locative/benefactive phrase بر سور ('for the feast/banquet') attached to این خانه ('this house').
segments.s612-g188: This short clause consists of two predicates joined by و. پدر میزبانست is a nominal clause: پدر (the father) + میزبانست (میزبان + ـست, 'is the host'). گنجور تست repeats the older/poetic possessive pattern: گنجور ('treasury') + تست (archaic contraction = 'توست' = 'is yours').
segments.s612-g189: This is an optative/blessing using باد as an optative particle ('may ... be'). شبان سیه is a poetic phrase: شبان is a literary/archaic plural (here 'nights') modified by سیه ('black/dark'); بر تو ('upon you/for you') marks the beneficiary, and فرخنده باد = 'may it be auspicious.'
segments.s612-g190: سرت برتر از ابر بارنده باد is a comparative blessing: سرت ('your head') + برتر از ('higher/superior than') + ابر بارنده ('a rain‑bearing cloud'). The final باد is again the optative particle 'may it be', so the whole line = 'May your head be higher than a rain‑bearing cloud.'
segments.s612-g191: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = to him/unto him; the verb گفت follows). بنشین و بردار چنگ are two imperatives joined by و: بنشین ('sit down') and بردار چنگ ('take the harp'), with the object چنگ following the second imperative.
segments.s612-g192: This is an impersonal necessity construction: یکی چامه (literally 'one poem') is the thing required, باید is the impersonal/modal 'it is necessary' and مرا is the dative/experiencer ('for me' = 'I need'). را here attaches as the object marker to the definite object, and بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'immediately/at once'. The whole means 'I need a poem immediately.'
segments.s612-g313: 'بیامد' is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb meaning "he came." The prepositional phrase 'بر خفته' (بر + خفته) follows it and means "to/upon the sleeper." 'برسان گرد' is a literary sequence in which 'برسان' (bring/approach) with the verbal/poetic particle 'گرد' conveys moving up to or causing someone to be brought/approached — overall: "he came to the sleeper and went to bring/rouse him."
segments.s612-g314: The noun phrase 'سر پیر' (literally "the old man's head") is a frequent poetic way to refer to the old man himself. 'از خواب' is a prepositional phrase "from sleep," and 'بیدار کرد' is a causative/perfect-like sequence (بیدار + کرد) meaning "made awake" = "woke (him)." The verb 'کرد' here functions as the light verb completing 'بیدار.'
segments.s612-g315: 'بدو گفت' is a standard word order in older Persian for "he said to him" (به + او). The verbs 'برخیز' and 'بگشای' are imperatives formed with the b-prefix (literary imperative), meaning "stand up" and "open/extend"; 'دست' is the object "hand." The conjunction 'و' links the two imperatives.
segments.s612-g316: The clause begins with the negator 'نه' (= "not"). 'هنگام خوابست' literally "it is the time of sleep" with 'خوابست' functioning as a copular/adjectival phrase "(is) sleep/for sleeping." 'و جای نشست' is parallel and elliptical: 'جای' = "place/seat" and 'نشست' here denotes sitting/seat — together: "not a time for sleeping nor a place to sit."
segments.s612-g317: 'که' introduces the content of what was spoken ("that/for"). 'شاه جهان' is an epithet "king of the world," and the copula is expressed in the older poetic form (here written as 'جهانست' attached). 'مهمان تو' (= "your guest") is a genitive phrase where 'تو' marks possession.
segments.s612-g318: The phrase 'بدین' (here written as 'ب' + 'دین') means "in/with this" (literary 'به + این' = "in this"), followed by 'بی‌نوا' (an epithet meaning "poor/wretched") modifying 'خانه' (= "house"). The coordinating 'و' links to 'مان تو' — 'مان' is an archaic poetic word meaning a dwelling/abode — and 'تو' marks possession: "this wretched house and your abode."
segments.s612-g319: 'یکایک' is an adverb meaning "one by one / each and every." It modifies the following noun phrase 'دل مرد گوهرفروش' where 'دل' = "heart," 'مرد' = "man/person" and 'گوهرفروش' = "gem-seller/jeweler," i.e., "each heart of the jewel-seller(s)." The word order is literary but straightforward: adverb + noun phrase.
segments.s612-g320: 'ز گفتار دربان' is an ablative/cause phrase "from the gatekeeper's speech/words." 'برآمد' is the past tense "arose/issued forth," and the predicative phrase 'به جوش' (literally "to/into boil") is a metaphorical state meaning "was inflamed/boiled with passion/anger." Together: "from the gatekeeper's words there rose (in them) a boiling/ardor."
segments.s612-g169: هم‌اکنون is a fronted temporal adverb meaning “right now.” بدین here functions as “by this / with this” (classical b- + demonstrative/obligation sense) introducing the pledge; با تو is the prepositional phrase “with you.” پیمان is the noun “pact/pledge” and کنم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive form of کردن placed at the end (typical Persian word order: complements before the verb) meaning “I make” or “I pledge.”
segments.s612-g170: به is the preposition “to/for”; بهرام is the proper name Bahram. شاهت is شاه + enclitic -ت “your king” (the possessive is attached to شاه). گروگان is the object “hostage/pledge,” and کنم (same verb as above) is the 1st‑person verb placed final; together گروگان کردن means “to make/take as hostage.”
segments.s612-g171: فراوان is an adverb “plentifully/heartily.” بخندید is the 3rd-person past of خندیدن “he laughed.” ز + او (written here as زو) is the classical/poetic “at/from him” (ز = from/at, combined with the pronoun), indicating the target of the laughter. شهریار is the subject “the king,” placed after the verb (classical word order allows verb before subject). Note: one token in the provided list (w3) appears orthographically mismatched to the expected pronoun.
segments.s612-g172: بدو is the dative phrase “to him/unto him.” گفت is the past verb “said” (here used to introduce direct speech); in the supplied sense list the expected verbal sense is missing, so it is noted below. نامم is نام + enclitic ـم “my name,” where the possessive is attached; گشسپ is the proper name Goshasp; سوار is an appositive noun “rider/horseman.” Word order: indirect object (بدو) + verb + complement (نامم ...).
segments.s612-g173: من is the explicit subject “I.” ایدر is an archaic/literary adverb meaning “here.” به + آواز چنگ is a prepositional phrase “at the sound/voice of the harp” (به marking cause or occasion), and آمدم is the 1st-person past “I came.” The clause thus states reason/circumstance (hearing the harp) before the verb.
segments.s612-g174: نه is the negation “not.” از بهر is a classical compound meaning “for the sake of / on account of” (از + بهر), جای is “place/position,” درنگ is the noun/verb “delay/tarry,” and آمدم is “I came.” The construction literally means “Not for the sake of place did I come to tarry,” i.e. “I did not come to stay for a position.”
segments.s612-g175: بدو is the dative “to him/unto him.” میزبان is the noun “the host.” گفت is the past verb “said” (introducing reported speech); in the supplied senses the appropriate verbal sense is not present. کاین is the classical demonstrative “this/that” (often equivalent to که + این), and دخترم is دختر + enclitic ـم “my daughter.” The clause introduces the host’s identification of his daughter.
