segments.s612-g176: همی is a classical particle often used as an intensifier or to mark an ongoing/future sense in older Persian (“will/indeed/constantly”). به آسمان اندر آرد is a verb phrase with goal first (به آسمان = “into the sky/heaven”) and آرد (3rd-singular ‘brings/raises’) final; together they mean “she will lift/bring (something) into the sky.” سرم here is intended as سر + ـم “my head” (literal “my head” used idiomatically for honor/status), but the supplied senses list does not match that reading, so its specific listed senses are not applicable. The whole clause means “she will raise my head to heaven,” i.e. exalt me.
segments.s612-g273: پسند منست is a poetic inversion meaning 'it is my liking' or 'it pleases me' (literally 'liking is mine/appropriate'); منست functions like a copular/predicate adjective. امشب and این چنگ‌زن follow to specify time and the thing liked: 'this harpist tonight.' Word order (predicate before modifiers) is typical of Persian poetic diction.
segments.s612-g274: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. این فال بد is the object phrase 'this bad omen' (فال = omen, بد = unfavorable). تا توانی is a subordinate extent clause 'as far as you can / as long as you are able' (تا = 'as far as', توانی = 'you can'). مزن at the end is a negative imperative 'do not strike/play' (here 'do not play [the omen/music]').
segments.s612-g275: چنین گفت is 'thus he said' (چنین = thus/so; گفت = 'said' here). با دخترش uses با in the sense 'to/with' addressing 'his daughter' (دخترش). آرزوی is the noun 'wish' and serves as the object/theme of what he expressed.
segments.s612-g276: پسندیدی is a verbal form meaning 'you liked/approved' (second-person perfective/past in poetic narrative or a present perfect sense). او را uses او with the object marker را to mark 'him/her' as the definite object. به گفتار و خوی means 'by/according to speech and disposition' with به functioning adverbially ('by') and گفتار و خوی listing the grounds (speech and manners).
segments.s612-g277: بدو گفت = 'he said to him/her' (بدو = to him/her; گفت = said). آری is the interjection 'yes/indeed.' پسندیده‌ام is a perfective first-person singular 'I have liked' (پسندیده + ـام), expressing completed feeling 'I like/have approved.'
segments.s612-g278: به جان و به دل هست is an idiomatic predication 'it is in my life and in my heart' (به ... به ... marking instruments/affections; هست = copula 'is'), stressing sincerity. چون دیده‌ام is a subordinate clause 'since/because I have seen (him/her)' (چون = 'since/because' here; دیده‌ام = perfect 'I have seen'). Note the verb at the end, a standard Persian clause order.
segments.s612-g279: بکن کار is an imperative sequence: بکن 'do/make' followed by its object کار 'the work/affair' — 'do your part.' زان پس is an adverbial phrase 'after that' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then'). به یزدان سپار is another imperative 'entrust to God' (سپار = 'entrust', with به یزدان marking the recipient).
segments.s612-g280: نه at the start negates the following nominal clause. گردون is the subject 'the sky/heaven' or poetically 'fate,' and جنگست is the predicate 'is at war' (جنگ + است fused as جنگست, a classical poetic form). با ماهیار is the oblique phrase 'with Mahyar' indicating the adversary; overall: 'The heavens/fate are not at war with Mahyar.'
segments.s612-g177: همو is an emphatic third‑person reference meaning 'the same one' (referring back to the woman); it precedes the predicate for emphasis. میگسارست is an archaic/descriptive predicative form 'is (a) cupbearer' (mi‑ prefix + noun + -st copula). و هم introduces a coordinated phrase with هم functioning as 'also' before the following noun چنگ‌زن, a compound meaning 'harp‑player/harpist.'
segments.s612-g178: همان here points back to the same person ('that very one' / 'she too'). چامه گویست is a noun + verbal copula construction meaning 'is a singer of poems/odes' (گویست is an archaic third‑person form built on 'گو' meaning 'to sing/say'). و links a second predicate: لشکر شکن is a compound where شکن ('breaker') modifies لشکر ('host/army') — 'breaker of hosts.'
segments.s612-g179: The structure 'X را Y نام بود' means 'X was named Y' — را marks دلارام as the definite/topic element and links it to the predicate 'آرزو نام بود' ('was named Arzū'). نام (name) plus بود (past copula) gives the naming in past tense; this is a conventional classical Persian word order (object/topic + predicate).
segments.s612-g180: همو again refers back to the same person ('she'). میگسار appears as a predicate noun 'cupbearer' with the copula omitted (a common poetic economy); the following و دلارام بود repeats the name with بود as an explicit past copula ('and she was Dularam'), an appositive-like restatement.
segments.s612-g181: به + noun (به سرو سهی) marks the indirect object or addressee — 'to the dark cypress' (سرو سهی is a poetic epithet: سرو 'cypress' + سهی 'dark/tawny'). گفت is the past‑tense verb 'said' introducing direct speech (here rendered as an imperative). بردار is the imperative 'take up!' and چنگ is its direct object 'the harp.' Word order is typical Classical Persian: indirect object → verb → imperative clause content.
segments.s612-g182: به پیش = 'before/in the presence of' and introduces the person addressed (گشسپ, Goshasp). آی here is an imperative vocative 'come!' (or an exclamation calling someone forward). با بوی و رنگ is a prepositional phrase 'with scent and color/ornament' modifying the imperative (how she should come), with بوی 'scent' and رنگ 'color/appearance/adornment.'
segments.s612-g183: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came' (perfective). بر پادشا = 'before/into the presence of the king' with بر marking direction/position. چنگ زن is an agentive compound 'harp‑player' where چنگ is the instrument and زن (from زدن 'to strike') functions as an agent noun 'one who plays/strikes' — together they identify who came.
segments.s612-g184: خرامان is an adverb/adjectival participle meaning 'strutting/proudly' (describing manner of coming). بسان introduces a simile 'like/as.' بت برهمن is a two‑word noun phrase: بت 'idol' and برهمن 'Brahmin' — together forming the image 'like a Brahmin's idol' (a poetic simile comparing her carriage to a statue/idol).
segments.s612-g385: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning “when.” بشنید is a 3rd‑person past tense (he/she/it heard) with its subject postponed; in poetry the verb can precede its subject, so دختر is the (pro‑drop) subject that follows. پدر را uses the object marker را to mark پدر as the direct object of بخواند; بخواند is 3rd‑person past of خواندن here meaning “call/summon.” The overall clause reads “When (she) heard, the daughter called the father.”
segments.s612-g386: همی is an emphatic/progressive particle (here conveying a continuing or immediate result: “was left/stood”). از دل is an ablative/genitive phrase “from/in the heart (of),” with شاه as the possessor. خیره is a predicative adjective meaning “stunned/ staring,” and بماند is 3rd‑person past / resultative “remained/was left.” Together: “(he) was left staring, stunned in his heart.”
segments.s612-g387: بیامد is the simple past “came.” پدر is the subject. دست کرده is a participial/periphrastic construction (“having set/with his hand placed”); کرده is the perfect participle turning دست into a stative phrase. به introduces the target (here ‘to/on the کش’), and کش in this line is a noun (sword/scabbard) rather than the verbal senses given, so the lemma senses list does not match exactly.
segments.s612-g388: به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning “into the presence of / before.” شهنشاه is the royal title “Shahanshah / emperor,” and خورشیدفش is an epithet or proper name attached to the king. The phrase therefore means that he approached/moved into the presence of the Shahanshah Khurshidafsh.
segments.s612-g389: بدو گفت is the common formula “he said to him” (بدو = to him; گفت = said). شاها is a vocative “O king!” ردا بخردا is an object plus dative/vocative: ردا “the robe,” بخردا addressed/directed ‘to the wise’ (the -ا ending here is an archaic dative/vocative form). The clause is an address ordering the giving of the robe to the wise.
segments.s612-g390: This segment is a string of vocatives/honorifics: بزرگا “great ones” (or ‘the great’), سترگا an epithet ‘bright‑eyed/with bright eyes,’ then گوا used as ‘witness’ or an imperative ‘bear witness,’ and موبدا addressing the mobed (priest). The line is elliptical—several addressees/titles followed by the exhortation to witness addressed to the priest.
segments.s612-g391: کسی ... دارد is a general relative/indefinite clause “one who has ….” Here کو functions as a relative pronoun “who/that” (an archaic form). خرد دارد = “possesses wisdom.” و connects to باهشی, which in context means a related quality (‘discernment/insight’), so the whole phrase reads “anyone who has wisdom and discretion.” Some lemmas in the list are mismatched to these classical forms, so the mapping is accordingly limited.
segments.s612-g392: نباید is an impersonal modal “one should not / must not.” It is followed by the infinitive گزیدن “to choose.” جز از combines to mean “except from/by,” and خامشی “silence” is the noun governing the exception. The clause therefore prescribes that a wise person should not choose except by silence (i.e. only in/through silence).
segments.s612-g209: ز is a preposition meaning 'because of / from' placed before the noun (prepositional phrase ز + مهمان). چنان ... که is a correlative 'so ... that' construction; چنان ('so') modifies شاد ('happy'), and گشتم is the past form of گشتن here meaning 'I became' (first‑person, past). که introduces the result clause headed by شاه.
segments.s612-g210: This is the result clause introduced by که. به جنگ is a directional prepositional phrase ('into/toward battle'), and the prosthetic ا (alef) before ندرون is a metrical/phonetic letter (written to make the word ʻاَندرونʼ); ندرون means 'inside/within'. چیره is an adjective/participle meaning 'dominant/victorious' and بیند is the (present) form of دیدن 'to see' used here ('he sees'/'he perceived'), with سپاه as its object 'the army'.
segments.s612-g211: چو این گفته شد is a temporal clause: چو = 'when', این گفته = 'this (having been) said' (گفته is a past participle), and شد is the auxiliary 'was' (passive/impersonal sense). سوی مهمان گذشت is a simple clause with سوی + noun 'toward the guest' and گذشت (past of گذشتن) meaning 'he passed/went'. The subject is omitted and supplied from context.
segments.s612-g212: ابا here functions adverbially ('with reluctance' / 'reluctantly'); چامه ('poem, ode') and چنگ ('harp') form a coordinated noun phrase joined by و, and نالان is a postpositive adjective/participle 'lamenting' describing the instrument. گذشت repeats the verb 'passed' (past of گذشتن). Word order places the manner/adverb (ابا) before the nouns and the modifier after the noun (نالان).
segments.s612-g213: به مهمان چنین گفت is a standard object‑first order: به + indirect object 'to the guest', چنین 'thus', and گفت here stands for spoken speech ('he said'). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; شاه‌فش is an epithet/compound vocative (a poetic title). Note that Persian often places the addressee after the verb or introduces the vocative immediately after a reporting clause.
segments.s612-g214: This segment lists epithets in apposition to the vocative: بلنداختر ('high‑rising / lofty‑starred') is a compound adjective, followed by coordinated attributes linked with و. یک‌دل is a prefixed compound meaning 'single‑hearted' (i.e., steadfast/united in heart), and کینه‌کش is a noun/adjective 'avenger' or 'one who bears/acts on resentment'. The sequence is typical of poetic enumeration of qualities.
segments.s612-g215: کسی 'someone/anyone' begins an indefinite interrogative; کو is an archaic relative/interrogative particle here ('who/that' in classical Persian), and ندیدست is a perfect/negative formation meaning 'has not been seen' or 'has not seen' depending on construction — here it yields 'who has not seen'. بهرام را places Bahram in the object position with the emphatic/definite marker را.
segments.s612-g216: خنیده is a past participle of خواندن 'to sing' used attributively before سوار ('the rider/horseman'): 'the rider who sang'. دلارام را is the object of خنیده (the one sung to) with the object marker را. In Persian participial modifiers often precede the noun they modify in poetry, forming 'خنیده سوار' = 'the rider who sang'.
segments.s612-g433: بیامد is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person singular verb 'came'. The preposition بر with پادشا (an archaic form of 'پادشاه') means 'to / before the king', and روزبه (a proper name) appears after the verb as the subject—poetic inversion places the name postverbally.
segments.s612-g434: گزیدند is a past, 3rd‑person plural 'they chose'. جایی is the thing chosen ('a place'); مر here is an archaic dative/benefactive clitic meaning roughly 'for' (not listed among the provided senses, so given -1), followed by او را, where او is the referent 'him' and را is the direct‑object/definiteness marker. به introduces the destination 'to' (به ده = 'to the village').
segments.s612-g435: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb often used as 'said' or 'ordered' (here 'ordered/commanded'). The name بهرام follows the verb as the agent, a common poetic inversion; خادم together with the numeral (چهل, 'forty') denotes 'forty attendants' (in classical poetry numerals often follow or modify the noun).
segments.s612-g436: همه is an intensifying/collective 'all' describing the attendants. ماه‌چهر ('moon‑faced') and دلگسل (a poetic adjective meaning 'heart‑seizing' or 'heart‑stealing') are attributive adjectives; و simply links the two adjectival phrases. The phrase as a whole describes the forty attendants as uniformly beautiful and captivating.
segments.s612-g437: رخِ رومیان is a genitive construction 'the faces of the Romans' (رخ = 'face' + رومیان = 'the Romans'). همچو introduces a simile 'like', and دیبای روم ('the brocade of Rome') is the image being compared to their faces—an artful poetic comparison.
segments.s612-g438: از ایشان ('from/of them' or 'because of them') gives the causal/agentive idea. همی is an archaic adverbial particle adding emphasis or continuity before the verb. تازه شد is a compound meaning 'became new / was renewed', and مرز و بوم ('border and dwelling') is a fixed pair meaning 'frontier and land' or 'realm'—the phrase means the realm was refreshed/renewed on their account.
segments.s612-g439: بشد آرزو is an impersonal/experiential phrase 'a wish arose' or 'it became a desire' (بشد here = 'it happened / it became'). تا introduces the subordinate clause expressing the wished‑for result ('that/so that'). به مشکوی is an adjective phrase 'musk‑scented' placed before شاه (the king), so شاه is the subject of the subordinate clause.
segments.s612-g440: نهاده is the past participle/verb 'placed / put (on)'. به سر بر is a prepositional phrase 'upon the head' (به + سر with the emphatic/poetic preposition بر). ز here means 'of' (poetic/genitive use) linking گوهر کلاه 'a crown of jewels'—so the clause says the king placed on his head a jewel‑decked cap/crown.
segments.s612-g249: به گفتار دختر بسنده نکرد is a negative past-clause: بسنده کردن (to be satisfied/ content) with an object marked by به + noun. Word order is prepositional phrase (به گفتار دختر) + verbal predicate (بسنده نکرد). نکرد is the simple past negative of کردن and makes the whole clause ‘was not satisfied with’ or ‘did not suffice with’.
segments.s612-g250: This is reported speech with an introductory ditransitive verb: به بهرام گفت = “he said to Bahram.” ای سوار is a vocative (O, rider). The final word نبرد here stands alone as the content of the utterance; in context it functions as the noun ‘battle’ (or an exclamatory reference to fighting) rather than as a conjugated verb.
segments.s612-g251: به ژرفی نگه کن سراپای اوی is an imperative clause. نگه کن is the verbal imperative formed by the noun/germ نگه (gaze/watch) plus the light verb کن (do!), so together it means ‘look’ or ‘pay heed.’ به ژرفی is an adverbial phrase ‘deeply/thoroughly,’ and سراپای اوی is a genitival phrase (‘from head to toe of her’) where اوی is the older/poetic possessive form meaning ‘her/his.’
segments.s612-g252: همان دانش و کوشش و رای اوی is a noun-list with a demonstrative/identifying modifier. همان (= ‘that/the same’) modifies the coordinated nouns دانش، کوشش، رای (knowledge, effort, counsel). اوی is the possessive pronoun meaning ‘hers’ (or ‘his’) and attaches after the list to show these qualities belong to her.
segments.s612-g253: نگه کن بدو تا پسند تو هست is another imperative + subordinate clause. نگه کن بدو = ‘look at her’ (imperative verb + indirect object). تا introduces the subordinate clause of extent/condition here meaning ‘as long as’ or ‘until,’ and پسند تو هست literally ‘your liking exists’ = ‘until you are pleased.’
segments.s612-g254: ازو آگهی بهترست ار نشست is a conditional statement: ازو آگهی بهترست = ‘there is better information/you will know better about her,’ where ازو = ‘about/from her’ and آگهی = ‘information/knowledge.’ ار (= اگر) introduces the condition ‘if’ and نشست is the simple past/verb form here meaning ‘she sits’ or ‘she were to sit’ (i.e. ‘if she sits down’), so the clause means ‘you’ll learn more of her if she sits.’
segments.s612-g255: بدین نیکوی نیز درویش نیست uses بدین (= به + این, ‘in/with this’) to point to the quality just described, نیکوی = ‘goodness/excellence,’ and نیز = ‘also/too.’ درویش نیست is idiomatic here: درویش (‘poor; lacking’) plus نیست = ‘is not lacking’ or ‘is not poor in’ — so the phrase means ‘she is not lacking in this excellence either’ (i.e. she too possesses this quality).
segments.s612-g256: به گفتن مرا رای کم‌بیش نیست is a comment about the speaker’s resolve: به گفتن = ‘by/to saying’ or ‘as for saying,’ مرا (here representing ‘me’ / ‘myself’ as object) and رای = ‘opinion/decision.’ کم‌بیش نیست means ‘is not more-or-less’ or ‘is not subject to variation.’ Together, the clause says that the speaker’s opinion will not be altered by what is merely said — ‘my decision won’t vary with talk.’
segments.s612-g57: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a third‑person past narrative 'he heard' (poetic order puts the verb before the subject). بهرام is the subject name. بالای خواست is a small predicate phrase: بالای is a poetic noun/adjective meaning 'highness/exaltation' or 'the high place', and خواست is past 'desired/wanted' — together 'he desired exaltation.'
segments.s612-g58: یکی جامه = 'one/a certain robe' (یکی as an indefinite numeral/determiner before the noun). خسرو آرای is a descriptive/genitive phrase meaning 'Khosrow‑adorned' / 'of Khosrow' (i.e. a royal/kingly garment). خواست again is the past verb 'he wanted/asked for' and is the main verb taking the preceding noun phrase as its object.
segments.s612-g59: جدا شد is a verbal phrase 'he parted/withdrew' (شد as past auxiliary/verb). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' governing دستور 'command/office'. وز here is actually a contracted sequence 'و ز' = 'and from' before لشکرش ('his army'); the provided lemma/senses for وز do not match this conjunctive+preposition use, so treat it as the fused 'and from'. The word order (verb then prepositional phrases) is typical of epic/poetic narrative.
segments.s612-g60: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed'. پر از آرزو is a predicative phrase 'full of desire' (پر 'full' + از 'of' + آرزو 'desire'). شد is 'became' (past). سرش = سر + ش (head + his) is the possessed noun 'his head' — so literally 'indeed his head became full of desire.'
segments.s612-g61: چنین 'thus/so' introduces reported speech. گفت here functions as the past tense verb 'he said' (the supplied senses list gives meanings like 'conversation' rather than the verbal past, so that supplied sense is not applicable). با موبدان روزبه is a prepositional phrase 'to/with the mobeds (the priests), Roozbeh' — موبدان is plural or collective of موبد and روزبه is the priest's name.
segments.s612-g62: که introduces the content clause 'that'. اکنون 'now/at present' sets the time. شود is the present/subjunctive form of 'become' used in reported or prospective sense ('will/shall become' or 'becomes'). شاه ایران is the predicate nominative 'king of Iran'. به is the preposition beginning the following phrase (here its exact relationship to the noun ده that follows is idiomatic/poetic), so grammatically it links the clause to the adverbial/locative element that follows.
segments.s612-g63: نشنید here is past negative/negation of hearing or 'was not told/did not hear' (third‑person past). بدان is written as two pieces in the annotation (ب + دان) but functions idiomatically as 'of/that' ('ب' = about/to, 'دان' as a pronominal/old form) — the provided senses do not neatly match this fused usage so the individual pieces are uncertain. خان گوهر فروش is a noun phrase 'the khan who is a gem‑seller' (خان title + گوهر 'gem' + فروش 'seller').
segments.s612-g64: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject. سوی 'toward' indicates direction (everyone turned toward the speech). گفتار 'speech' is the object/goal. دارید is present plural 'have' (here part of the idiom 'گوش داشتن' = 'to lend an ear / to pay attention'), and گوش 'ear' is used idiomatically as 'attention/listening'. Word order places the directional phrase before the predicate idiom.
segments.s612-g113: چنین is a prepositive demonstrative meaning “such/thus.” The noun phrase اسپ و زرین ستامی links two coordinated items with و (and). به کوی is a prepositional phrase indicating location (“into/in the quarter”). Note that زرین (=“golden”) modifies the following noun ستامی; the exact meaning of ستامی is poetic/archaic here and functions as part of the noun phrase.
segments.s612-g114: بدزدد is a classical present/subjunctive-form verb meaning “steal / should steal” used to express a hypothetical or potential action; the verb appears before its indefinite subject کسی (typical older Persian word order). من شوم uses the subjunctive شوم (“I would become / may become”) to give the speaker’s consequent state, and چاره‌جوی is a predicative noun meaning “seeker of remedy/help.”
segments.s612-g115: بیامد is simple past “came.” The phrase places the subject (کنیزک, “the young maid/maidservant”) before the directional phrase به دهقان (“to the peasant”), and ends with the past-report verb بگفت (“said/told”) — a classical narrative sequence: “she came … and said.”
segments.s612-g116: که introduces reported speech or a subordinate clause (“that”). مردی is an indefinite subject “a man.” همی is the classical progressive/emphatic particle (marks ongoing or imminent action here) and خواهد is an auxiliary expressing future/intention (“will/is going to”); از ما is a prepositional phrase “from us,” and نهفت is the verb ‘hide/conceal’ (here meaning ‘(is) hiding himself from us’).
segments.s612-g117: همی is the progressive/emphatic particle meaning roughly “keeps / is (constantly).” گوید is the present-tense speech verb “says” (introducing the reported utterance). The noun phrase اسپی به زرین ستام places اسپی (“steed/horse”) with a following modifier introduced by به + adjective (here used instrumentally or linking: “with/with…”) زرین (“golden”) + ستام (a poetic noun; here part of the object being described). This segment begins the quoted report.
segments.s612-g118: بدزدند is third-person plural present/subjunctive ‘they steal / would steal’ used for reported potential action. از ایدر is a locative phrase ‘from here.’ شود is a present/future auxiliary ‘will become / will happen,’ and کار خام is an idiomatic predicate meaning the affair will go badly / the business will turn out raw/unripe (i.e. ill). The clause is a prediction or warning about consequences.
segments.s612-g119: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed narrative formula: چنین (‘thus/so’) + داد (past ‘gave’) + پاسخ (‘answer’) = “he answered thus.” The conjunction که introduces the content of the answer, and بگشای در is an imperative-like form (‘open the door’), where بگشای is a classical present/imperative verb form and در here means “door.”
segments.s612-g120: به بهرام گفت = ‘he said to Bahram’ (به marks the indirect object/recipient). اندر introduces direction/place (‘into/in’), and آی‌ای is a vocative/imperative ‘Come!’ (the doubled form is emphatic or vocative). پسر is the vocative noun ‘O son’ or ‘son’ used addressingly. The sequence reports a second utterance addressed directly to Bahram.
segments.s612-g201: پدر را is the indirect-object construction 'to (his) father', with را marking the definite/target; چنین گفت is 'said thus' (چنین modifying گفت). کای is the vocative particle 'O' placed before the name ماهیار, so the whole segment introduces quoted speech addressed to Māhyār.
segments.s612-g202: چو introduces a simile 'as/like'; سرو سهی is a poetic compound 'a dark/tall cypress' (سرو 'cypress' modified by the epithet سهی 'dark'); بر لب جویبار is a locative phrase 'upon the lip/edge of the stream' with بر 'on' governing لب 'edge' and جویبار 'stream'. The phrase functions as the simile completing the quoted line.
segments.s612-g203: چو here = 'as/when' introducing the comparison. کافور کرده سر is a poetic participial phrase literally 'having made (her) head camphor' (کرده as a perfective/participial modifier of سر), and مشکبوی is a postpositive adjective 'musk‑scented' describing that head.
segments.s612-g204: This is a coordinated description: زبان گرم‌گوی = 'a tongue that speaks warmly/fluently' (گرم‌گوی a compound adjective), و = 'and', دل آزرم جوی = 'a heart that seeks Azram' (جوی here functioning as 'seeking/searching'). Word order places descriptive compounds and the coordinating و between the two qualities.
segments.s612-g205: همیشه 'always/ever' sets the temporal scope; بداندیشت is 'your ill‑wisher' (بداندیش + enclitic -ت 'your'), آزرده is the predicate adjective 'distressed', and باد is the optative particle 'may' (a wish/formulaic curse). The clause is therefore a wishing formula: 'May your enemy always be distressed.'
segments.s612-g206: به دانش is a prepositional phrase 'by/with knowledge', روان تو 'your soul/mind' is the patient/subject of the wish, پرورده is a past‑participle used adjectivally 'nurtured/cultivated', and the final باد again marks the optative 'may it be'. The whole clause = 'May your soul be nurtured by knowledge.'
segments.s612-g207: توی is a poetic/archaic form here functioning as the second‑person topic 'you' (or 'among you'); چون 'like/as', فریدون a proper name, آزاده خوی = 'noble/free disposition' (آزاده modifying خوی). The clause compares the addressee to Fereydun in character.
segments.s612-g208: منم 'I am' (stressed form), چون 'like', پرستار used in the sense 'worshipper/devotee', نام آرزوی is a genitival phrase 'the name of Arzuy' or 'the name “Desire”' functioning as the object of devotion; the clause says the speaker is, like a devotee, attached to that name. Word order places the simile and the object after the predicate for poetic emphasis.
segments.s612-g337: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing a reported clause. Word order is poetic: subject (من) + time adverb (دوش 'last night') + prepositional element (پیش 'in the presence of') + noun (شهنشاه) and then the predicate adjective مست 'drunk' follows the noun, giving 'I last night, before the shah, [was] drunk.'
segments.s612-g338: چرا بودم here uses چرا as an adverbial/colloquial rhetorical 'how/why' introducing the state 'I was' (بودم, past of بودن). The conjunction و links that clause to the following clause whose subject is دخترم; می‌پرست is a present‑imperfective form meaning 'adores/admired/was admiring' with دخترم as its subject ('my daughter was admiring').
segments.s612-g339: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came' and سوی + noun marks direction ('toward'). حجره آرزوی is a genitive construction 'the chamber of Arzuy' (Arzuy is a proper name here, written as the noun آرزو with a possessive ending), so the whole phrase means '(he) came toward Arzuy's chamber.'
segments.s612-g340: بدو is the dative clitic 'to her/unto her' and گفته/گفت functions here as reporting 'said'. The vocative کای introduces direct address ('O ...'); ماه is a poetic vocative 'moon' meaning 'beautiful one'; آزاده‌خوی is a post‑positive adjective 'free‑spirited' or 'of noble disposition'.
segments.s612-g341: A simple nominal clause: شهنشاه بهرام بود 'the shah was Bahram.' The following آنک 'that/who' is a relative/connecting particle linking this identification to the next clause; دوش 'last night' is a time adverbial attached to that relative clause ('who, last night ...').
segments.s612-g342: بیامد 'came' takes سوی to mark motion toward a place; خان here means an inn/caravanserai (place) and گوهرفروش 'gem‑seller' names the owner/establishment: 'came to the gem‑seller's khan/inn.' Word order is verb + direction + place phrase.
segments.s612-g343: همی آمد is a poetically habitual/continuous past form meaning 'he was coming' or 'he came (in continuous way)'; از دشت نخچیرگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the plain of the hunting‑ground' indicating origin. Word order places the adverbial predicate before the origin phrase.
segments.s612-g344: عنان 'reins' is the grammatical subject that follows by the past archaic verb تافتست 'it/they shone, flashed' (tightly bound epic past). از کهن دژ به راه is a movement/location phrase 'from the ancient fortress onto the road'; taken together: 'the reins shone as he went from the old fortress onto the road.'
segments.s612-g225: دو بازو is a simple numeral + noun ('two arms'). به کردار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the manner/shape of' (کردار here = 'manner/appearance'); ران هیون is a genitival/attributive phrase 'the haunch (ران) of the beast (هیون)' with the noun-possessor order typical of Persian poetry. The whole segment describes the arms as shaped like a beast's haunch.
segments.s612-g226: به پای اندر آری is a locative phrase + verb: به پای اندر = 'at/to the foot/base' (اندر = 'in/within'); آری here is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'you put/place/bring' (second-person), so the clause means 'you set/placed ... at the foot'. که بیستون identifies or apposes that foot as 'Bisotun' (که introducing identification).
segments.s612-g227: This clause is an emphatic, poetic question/exclamation. تو is fronted for emphasis; آنی (literally 'instant/now') and کجا form the interrogative 'where (are you at this moment)'. چشم کس = 'any one's eye' is the logical subject, and چون تو مرد = 'a man like you' is the object/complement being negated later.
segments.s612-g228: ندید is the past negative 'did not see'; و نبیند uses نبیند (present/subjunctive negative) to express a general future/ever statement 'and will not see'. به‌روز نبرد is a temporal complement 'on the day of battle' (به‌روز = 'of the day', نبرد = 'battle'). The verb forms create the sense 'has not seen and will not see'.
segments.s612-g229: This is an optative/wish construction closed by باد ('may'). The sequence is elliptical: تن آرزو خاک پای تو باد = 'May my body be the longing; (may it be) the dust of your feet.' Grammatically باد is the optative particle that applies to the preceding noun phrases; خاک پای تو is a genitive phrase 'the dust of your feet.'
segments.s612-g230: همه‌ساله زنده برای تو باد is another optative clause: همه‌ساله 'yearly/every year' modifies زنده 'alive'; برای تو marks the beneficiary 'for you'; باد again is the optative 'may it be', giving the wish 'may you remain alive every year.' Word order places the temporal adverb before the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s612-g231: جهاندار is a vocative/noun 'sovereign' addressing the ruler. ازان 'from that' links backwards to what was said earlier; چامه و چنگ اوی = 'his poem and harp' (اوی is the archaic third-person possessive 'his/her'). The clause states that the poem and harp derive from that source.
segments.s612-g232: ز + a coordination of nouns: ز دیدار و بالا و آهنگ اوی = 'from (his) appearance/visage and loftiness and melody (they are)'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', the three nouns are listed in parallel, and the possessive اوی at the end governs the whole list.
segments.s612-g1: وزانجا is an adverbial phrase meaning 'from there' (literary compound). برانگیخت is a 3rd-person past transitive verb 'he roused/raised' that takes a direct object marked by را. شبرنگ را is the definite object (شبرنگ here used as a name/thing) with را as the object marker placed after the noun in the normal Persian order.
segments.s612-g2: بدیدش is the past tense 'he saw' with the enclitic -ش 'it/him' attached (verb + clitic object). یکی functions as 'a certain' (introducing an indefinite/one). بیشه تنگ را is the noun phrase 'a narrow thicket' with تنگ (narrow) post-modifying بیشه (grove/thicket) and را marking it as definite/obj; the clause overall: 'he saw a certain narrow thicket.'
segments.s612-g3: دو شیر ژیان is a noun phrase with the numeral دو 'two' + شیر 'lion' + ژیان functioning here as an adjectival/qualifying element (literary: 'living'/'vivid' → 'two live/ferocious lions'). پیش آن بیشه is the locative/adverbial 'in front of that thicket' (پیش = before/in front of; آن = that). دید is the 3rd-person past 'he saw' and completes the clause.
segments.s612-g4: کمان را is the definite object 'the bow' (را marks it). به زه کرد is a light/compound verbal phrase: به + زه (to the string) + کرد (did/made) — together meaning 'he strung the bow' (literally 'made [it] to the string'). و اندر کشید links with 'and' + اندر 'in/within' + کشید 'drew' — here 'drew (the bowstring) back'. Note: کشید 'to draw' (here) is the intended meaning.
segments.s612-g5: بزد is the past tense 'he struck/struck at'. تیر is the instrument/object 'an arrow'. بر سینه شیر is the locative 'upon the lion's breast' (بر = on/upon, سینه = breast, شیر = lion). چاک is a result/resultative adjective/noun 'split/gash' indicating the chest was torn by the blow.
segments.s612-g6: گذر کرد is 'it passed' (lit. 'made a passing' — the arrow passed through). تا introduces the result/purpose sense 'so that' here. پر و پیکان is a coordinated direct phrase 'the feather and the shaft/arrow'; به خاک marks direction/result 'to the ground' — together: 'it passed so that feather and arrow fell to the earth.'
segments.s612-g7: بر + ماده + شد is a literary sequence: بر + noun + شد can mean 'fell upon / turned upon the [noun]' or 'became with respect to the [noun]'; here 'ماده' = the she-animal (she-lion). تیز modifies the action meaning 'swift/fierce'. بگشاد دست is a pre-verbal perfective 'opened (her) hand/paw' (بگشاد with دست 'hand/paw'), so overall: '(she) became fierce and quickly opened her sharp paw.'
segments.s612-g8: بر شیر 'upon the lion' marks the target. با گردرانش is an instrumental/adiunct phrase 'with a turning/twist' (گردرانش is a literary noun related to turning/whirling). ببست is a past verb here meaning 'he/she clasped/seized/closed upon' (literary 'bound/tied/closed on'), so the clause says she, with a twist, gripped/closed upon the lion.
segments.s612-g129: گر is the conditional 'if'. افزون شود is a verbal phrase where افزون (in the sense 'become increased') combines with شود (3rd person singular 'become') — together 'become more' or 'are increased'. دانش و داد من is the coordinated subject 'my knowledge and my justice'; the possessive من attaches to the coordinated nouns and scopes over both.
segments.s612-g130: پس از = 'after' (پس + از). مرگ is the noun 'death'. روشن بود is a past copula construction (روشن + بود), literally 'was bright/clear' — here used to state that the speaker's یاد (remembrance/renown) will be conspicuous after death; یاد من is the subject 'my remembrance/name' placed after the predicate for poetic order.
segments.s612-g131: همه زیردستان is the plural subject 'all the vassals/subjects'. چو is the comparative/simile 'like/as', and گوهرفروش is the noun 'gem‑seller/jeweler' used as the point of comparison — the verb is omitted here and supplied in the next segment.
segments.s612-g132: بمانند is the 3rd person plural present form of ماندن with the b‑prefix (they remain/are left/they stay like). با + ناله چنگ و نوش is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the lament (ناله) of the harp (چنگ) and the drink/wine (نوش)', giving the manner in which they 'remain'.
segments.s612-g133: چو here means 'when'. آمد and رسید are two past verbs in sequence ('came' and 'reached'); به بالای ایوان is a prepositional phrase 'to the top of the iwan/portico' modifying رسید. The doubled verb sequence is a common poetic narrative pattern.
segments.s612-g134: ز is the poetic form of 'from' (here 'from the door' / 'at the doorway'). در in this line denotes 'door/gate'. دختر میزبان is 'the host's daughter' (possessor following the noun), را is the definite object marker attached to that noun phrase, and بدید is the past 'saw' — object + را precedes the verb, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s612-g135: چو = 'when'. دهقان is the subject 'the peasant' (or rural gentleman), ورا = 'him/to him' is the object of دید ('saw'). بر پای خاست is a past action 'arose to his feet' (بر = on/upon, پای = foot, خاست = rose), meaning 'he stood up'. The clause sequence links seeing to the subsequent action of standing.
segments.s612-g136: بیامد = 'came forward' (past). خم آورد is a light-verb expression literally 'brought a bend' — idiomatically 'he bowed'. بالای راست indicates the place/side he went to: بالای = 'upper/atop' and راست = 'right', so 'to the right side (of the platform)'; the sequence = 'came, bowed, and took the right (place)'.
segments.s612-g353: چو introduces a temporal/conditional protasis 'when/if'. پرسدت is an archaic second-person past/conditional meaning 'you ask' or 'if you ask (him)'. با او is the prepositional phrase 'with him / to him', and سخن نرم‌گوی is an instruction to speak gently: نرم‌گوی is a compound adjective/imperative 'speak softly' placed with the noun سخن ('speech') so the clause reads literally 'when you ask him, speak soft words.' The verb-ish element نرم‌گوی functions as the main directive of the clause.
segments.s612-g354: سخنهای is the plural/collective 'words/speeches' and here marks the material to be uttered. با شرم و بازرم is a prepositional phrase 'with shame/modesty and ...' (شرم = modesty/shame). بازرم is an archaic/poetic item meaning a quality like restraint or reverence; its exact dictionary senses given do not match this usage (see senses field). گوی (here) is the verb 'say'/'utter' (a verbal noun/imperative sense), placed finally as in classical Persian, so the whole segment means 'utter words with modesty and restraint.'
segments.s612-g355: من اکنون نیایم is a present/near-future negative statement 'I will not come now' (نیایم from نیا 'to come' used in the negative sense). اگر introduces a conditional clause; خواندم here is an archaic past-form used idiomatically as 'if I am called / if I am summoned' (sense 'to call/summon'), so together it reads 'I will not come now, even if I am called.'
segments.s612-g356: به جای marks the locative/role 'to/in the place (of)'. پرستنده is the object 'a worshipper / devotee' and بنشاندم is a first-person past 'I seated / I placed.' The clause follows classical SOV tendencies: 'I seated a worshipper in (his) place.'
segments.s612-g357: بسان means 'like/as'. همالان (from همال) refers to carriers/porters or low attendants; here it means 'like the porters/servitors'. نشستم is the past first-person 'I sat' (classical past), and به خوان 'at the banquet/table' gives the location. The phrase as a whole: 'I sat at the table like the porters.'
segments.s612-g358: که introduces a clause of explanation or result ('that/for/because'). اندر تنم is the prepositional phrase 'in my body' (اندر = in; تنم = 'my body'—the pronoun is suffixed). خرد با استخوان is a compact phrase: خرد here is used in a concrete/physical sense (something small or meagre, i.e. little flesh) collocated with استخوان 'bone', so the clause means roughly 'for in my body there is little flesh with bone' (a poetic way to say the speaker is reduced to skin and bone).
segments.s612-g359: که introduces the clause (here causal/explanatory). من نیز 'I also' and گستاخ گشتم is a two-word predicate meaning 'I became bold/impudent' (گشتم = 'I became', گستاخ = 'impudent/bold'). به شاه is a dative/locative phrase 'toward the king / before the king', so the clause reads 'that I too became impudent toward the king.'
segments.s612-g360: به پیر و جوان is a paired phrase 'to/for the old and the young' (here meaning 'among old and young' or 'from old and young'). از می‌آید is the compound verb 'comes from' and گناه is the noun 'sin/fault'; classical word order places the verb phrase before the noun, so the line means 'sin comes from both old and young.'
segments.s612-g401: کسی را که می‌انده آرد به روی is a relative clause starting with کسی را 'someone (with object marker)'. که introduces the relative clause 'who/whom'. می‌انده (a classical/poetic present form) + آرد (present of آوردن 'to bring') together describe the action 'puts/sets (before him)'; به روی is a locative phrase 'to/onto the face/front' (i.e. 'before him' or 'in view'). Word order is object (کسی را) then relative clause describing what is done to that person.
segments.s612-g402: نباید که یابد ز می‌رنگ و بوی is an impersonal/modal construction: نباید 'one must not / it should not be' + که introduces the subordinate clause. یابد is the present/subjunctive/optative sense 'find/obtain' (here 'be found to have'). ز marks source 'from', and می‌رنگ و بوی is a coordinated nominal phrase 'the wine-colour and scent' (می‌رنگ = wine-color, بوی = scent). The whole clause says such a person should not be found to possess wine's color or smell.
segments.s612-g403: به مستی is a circumstantial phrase 'in/through drunkenness'. ندیدم is past tense, first person singular negative 'I have not seen'. ز before تو marks source/cause 'in you / coming from you'; بدخوی is a predicate/adjective 'ill-tempered'. Put together: 'I have not seen (any) ill temper in you caused by drunkenness.'
segments.s612-g404: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here 'still/indeed' or habitual/emphatic 'surely'); ز آرزو is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/out of desire/longing'; این سخن is the direct object 'this speech/these words'; بشنوی is second-person singular present/subjunctive (in classical style used like a future/you shall) 'you will hear' or 'may you hear'. The clause therefore promises emphatically that you will hear these words out of longing.
segments.s612-g405: تو is the explicit subject 'you'. پوزش بران کن is an idiomatic imperative: پوزش 'excuse/apology' + بران (an adverbial/formative element meaning 'thereon' or 'forth') + کن 'do' (imperative), i.e. 'make an excuse' or 'offer a pardon'. که + تا introduces a subordinate clause with تا 'until' (here 'until/so that when'); چنگ زن is a compound reading 'چنگ‌زن' (harp-player) though written as noun + زن (or read as 'strike the harp') — functionally it refers to the harpist or the act of playing the harp.
segments.s612-g406: بگوید here is a third-person singular present/subjunctive/epic-present form 'may speak / will say' used in narrative; it governs the following noun phrase as subject. همان لاله اندر سمن is a nominal phrase 'that same laleh (tulip/beautiful one) in the saman (jasmine/pleasant-scented flowers)', i.e. 'that same tulip among the jasmines'. Grammatically the line says the speaker should make excuses until that tulip (a poetic personification) speaks when the harp plays.
segments.s612-g407: بگوید یکی is a narrative clause 'someone says' (بگوید here functions as the narratorial 'says' or 'will say'); یکی 'someone/a certain one' is the subject. تا introduces the reported clause; بدان is the compound sense 'to that / to it' (here written as ب + دان) and می‌خوریم is present first-person plural 'we drink' (here 'we will drink / let us drink' as a toast). The structure reports a speaker proposing a toast: 'Someone says, "Let us drink to that."'
segments.s612-g408: پی روز is a temporal phrase 'after/on the day' or 'the day to come' (پی = after/following). ناآمده is a participial adjective 'not come / not yet come' (i.e. the future/unarrived day). نشمریم is first-person plural present-subjunctive/imperative negative 'let us not count / we will not count' (classical negative hortative/optative). Together: 'we will not count (i.e. we do not reckon on) the day that has not yet come.'
segments.s612-g417: چو (chū) is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when' and introduces a temporal clause. نان خورده شد is a perfect/passive-style clause ('the bread was eaten' or idiomatically 'the meal was finished') where خورده (past participle 'eaten') combines with شد (here functioning as auxiliary 'was'). آرزو را بخواند follows as the main action of the matrix clause: آرزو + را is the definite object marked by را, and بخواند (pre‑past/epic present of خواندن) means 'he pronounced/recited/voiced' — literally 'he read/uttered the wish.'
segments.s612-g418: به کرسی زر پیکرش برنشاند is a separate clause giving a following action. به + کرسی expresses direction/placement 'onto/onto the seat' (به used as 'towards/into'). کرسی زر = 'a seat/throne of gold' (زر qualifying کرسی). پیکرش 'his body/figure' is the object possessed by the enclitic ـش, and برنشاند (بر + نشاند, causative of نشستن) means 'he seated/placed (himself or the person) upon' — here 'set his form on a golden throne.'
segments.s612-g419: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense reporting verb here ('he said/commanded'). تا introduces a purpose/that‑clause ('so that/that'), so بفرمود تا … expresses 'he ordered that …'. چنگ برداشت is the subordinate action: چنگ 'harp' and برداشت (برداشتن in past) 'took up / lifted' — so 'take up the harp'. ماه here is a vocative/epithet 'the fair one / beauty' (classical usage), the agent who is to take up the harp.
segments.s612-g420: بدان چامه here functions as 'to/unto that poem' — بد (ب) is the prepositional element 'to/into' and دان attaches to produce the older compound 'بدان' meaning 'to that'. چامه means 'poem/ode'. کز پیش فرمود شاه is a relative clause modifying چامه: کز = 'which', پیش = 'before/previously', فرمود = 'the king had commanded/said' (past). Grammatically, the verb فرمود belongs to شاه (the king) and establishes that the poem had been ordered by the king earlier.
segments.s612-g421: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus/so', گفت here as 'said'). کای is the vocative marker 'O' (classical poetry), followed by شهریار دلیر 'brave sovereign' — شهریار 'king/sovereign' and دلیر 'brave' as an epithet addressed to the addressee.
segments.s612-g422: This clause is a relative/interrogative construction introduced by که ('who/that'): بگذارد is a present/subjunctive-hinted form of گذارد (to place/leave/let) used here in the sense 'would leave/abandon' or 'would allow to remain aside'; از marks source/relation 'from/of', نام تو = 'your name/reputation' (نام here = fame/renown), and بیشه شیر 'the lion's thicket' (بیشه 'grove/thicket', شیر 'lion') — together forming 'who would leave the lion's thicket from your name' (poetic phrasing about reputation).
segments.s612-g423: توی here functions as the older/formal vocative/identifying 'thou' (classical poetic form corresponding to توئی/تو), introducing an address to the addressee. شاه پیروز و لشکرشکن are epithets in apposition: شاه 'king', پیروز 'victorious', و 'and', لشکرشکن 'army-breaker' (a compound epithet meaning one who routs armies). The segment names and praises the addressee.
segments.s612-g424: همان رویه begins with همان 'that very/the same' referring back to the face/appearance; رویه (here) = 'aspect/face/appearance'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and لاله اندر چمن completes it: لاله 'tulip' (a conventional image of beauty) + اندر چمن 'in the meadow' — so 'that same countenance is like a tulip in the meadow.'
segments.s612-g9: چنین (thus) is an adverb placed before گفت (said) to introduce reported speech; گفت is a past-tense verb ‘he said’. کان here introduces the reported clause (classical/poetic use equivalent to English 'that' / 'as to the fact that'), followed by the nominal clause تیر بی‌پر بود ('the arrow was featherless'), where بی‌پر is a compound adjective 'without feathers' and بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s612-g10: نبد is an archaic negative form (contracted classical form of نبود/نبود) meaning 'there was not'; it negates the following NP تیز پیکان ('a sharp/keen arrow') where تیز is an adjective before the noun پیکان. او is the third-person pronoun 'he/it' referring back to the owner/agent, and کر بود is a predicate with the adjective کر 'deaf/unheeding' plus the past copula بود = 'was'. The clause therefore states a negation about sharp arrows and then attributes deafness.
segments.s612-g11: سپاهی is the plural/collective noun 'soldiers' serving as the subject. همی is a classical particle marking continuous or emphatic action (roughly 'kept on' / 'were'), and خواندند is the past plural form 'they called/cried' (here meaning 'they cried out'). آفرین is the exclamation/object of the cry ('bravo' / an acclamation).
segments.s612-g12: که‌ای is a classical vocative/address (که + ای) used to call out to someone: 'O ...'. نامور is an epithet 'renowned', شهریار is the addressee title 'sovereign/king', and زمین is the scope 'of the earth/land' (here part of the title). Word order is vocative followed by descriptive titles.
segments.s612-g13: ندید (past 'did not see') followed by و نبیند (present/subjunctive 'and will not see') is a classical rhetorical pair used to state a timeless negation: 'has not seen and will not see'. کسی is the indefinite 'anyone', and در جهان is the locative prepositional phrase 'in the world'. The doubled tense (past + present/subjunctive) gives a general, timeless assertion.
segments.s612-g14: چو is a comparative/conditional particle 'like/as', introducing the example that follows. تو is the vocative/subject 'you', شاه is an appellation 'king', بر تخت is a prepositional phrase 'upon the throne' (بر = on), and شاهنشهان is the genitive/title 'of shahanshahs (emperors)'. Together the phrase means 'like you, king, upon the throne of shahanshahs.'
segments.s612-g15: چو is here a conditional/simile 'as/when'. با introduces the instrumental phrase با تیر بی‌پر 'with a featherless arrow' (با + noun + compound adjective). تو is the subject 'you', and شیرافگنی is an epithet applied to 'you' (a compound adjective in Classical Persian); its exact lexical sense is marked in the entry but grammatically it functions as a predicative/epithetic adjective describing the subject.
segments.s612-g16: پی here is used in the spatial/schematic sense 'at the foot of / along' (classical uses include 'foot; track'), followed by the noun phrase کوه خارا 'the flinty/hard mountain' (کوه + epithet خارا). ز بن is a prepositional phrase 'from the root/base', and برکنی is a second-person singular present/subjunctive/optative form (بر + کنی) meaning 'you tear off/uproot'. The verb matches the earlier subject تو, giving 'you uproot/pull off from the root the (base of the) flinty mountain.'
segments.s612-g265: بیاریم is an optative/imperative‑style first‑person plural verb (classical form of بیاوریم) meaning “let us bring” or “we should bring.” پیران داننده is a noun phrase with داننده (a participial/attributive adjective = “knowing/knowledgeable”) modifying پیران (“elders”), and را is the definite object marker marking the whole phrase as the direct object of بیاریم.
segments.s612-g266: This is a second coordinated object (joined to the first by the sense of the verb). شکیبا دل is an adjectival phrase in which شکیبا (‘patient’) modifies دل (‘heart’) — literally “patient‑hearted.” و simply links items. چیز خواننده را is a noun phrase where چیز (‘matter/thing’ here = the reciter’s material/song) is specified by خواننده (‘the reciter/singer’); را again marks it as a definite object. The whole line lists things to be brought.
segments.s612-g267: شب تیره is a nominal phrase meaning “the dark/ominous night.” از ... بیرون بود uses the preposition از + بیرون + بود to mean “was outside of / contrary to” (i.e. not according to) رسم (custom). Here بود is the past copula: “was.”
segments.s612-g268: نه introduces a negative continuation: “nor / it was not.” آیین شاه آفریدون is a genitive construction ‘the ritual/custom of King Afrīdūn.’ بود again is the past copula: “was not.” The segment says that the night did not follow the established ritual of that king.
segments.s612-g269: نه فرخ بود negates: “it was not fortunate/auspicious.” مست زن خواستن is a nominal/verbal phrase: مست (‘drunk’) + زن (‘woman’) + خواستن (‘to desire/seek’) — here it expresses the idea of drunken revelry and pursuing women as an activity; the whole phrase is something that 'was not auspicious'.
segments.s612-g270: وگر نیز starts a contrasting conditional/adder: وگر (‘and/or if’) + نیز (‘also’). کاری نو آراستن is an infinitival/nominal phrase meaning “to set up / attempt a new undertaking” (کاری نو = a new matter/work; آراستن = to prepare/adorn/put in order). The line forbids both carousing/wooing and trying new ventures at that time.
segments.s612-g271: بدو گفت بهرام is an inverted word order common in classical Persian: بدو (‘to him’) + گفت (‘said’) + بهرام (subject) — “Bahram said to him.” کاین is a fused relative/ demonstrative (‘that this / that which’), and بیهده‌ست is a predicative adjective + copula meaning “is pointless/foolish.” Together: “Bahram said to him that this is foolish.”
segments.s612-g272: What follows بیهده‌ست is an infinitival list introduced without an overt particle: زدن here is the verbal infinitive ‘to strike/to sound/to perform.’ فال بد is a collocation ‘bad omen’ (i.e. an ill omen), رای و راه are two coordinated nouns ‘counsel/opinion and way/course,’ and به دست completes the phrase with the sense ‘into the hand / into (someone’s) control’ (i.e. placing the course into hands). Grammatically, the whole phrase is the thing declared بیهده‌ست — “it is foolish to (do) …”.
segments.s612-g105: بزد is the 3rd-person past of زدن used here as 'he struck/knocked'. حلقه را is a definite direct object (حلقه = 'ring/knocker') marked by the object marker را. بر در is a prepositional phrase 'upon the door' (بر + در 'door'); و links to the next phrase and then بار خواست uses بار (noun) plus خواست (past 'he desired/asked') — here بار is the noun being requested and خواست is the main past-tense verb for that clause.
segments.s612-g106: This clause reads as a compact subject–object–predicate line: خداوند (subject 'the lord/sovereign') + خورشید را (object 'the sun', marked by را) + یار خواست (predicate 'sought as a companion' — خواست is past 'sought/wanted' and یار functions as complement 'friend/ally'). Word order is typical Persian SOV with the object marker را on خورشید.
segments.s612-g107: پرستنده مهربان is a noun phrase 'the kind worshipper/devotee' where the adjective مهربان follows the noun (typical in Persian). گفت here functions as the reporting verb 'said' (past). کیست is the question 'who is it?' with کی = 'who' and ست the copula 'is'.
segments.s612-g108: This is an interrogative nominal clause formed with the infinitive/masdar زدن ('to strike/knock') plus the rest of the phrase and finalized by چیست ('what is it/what is the reason'). در here is 'door' (در زدن = 'to knock (at) a door'), شب تیره = 'dark night' (noun + adjective), and the idiomatic prepositional pair از بهر means 'for the sake/purpose of' (archaic). The clause literally asks 'To strike the door in the dark night, for what is it?' i.e. 'What is the reason for knocking at the door in this dark night?'
segments.s612-g109: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed poetic order meaning 'thus (چنین) gave (داد) an answer (پاسخ)' — داد is past 'gave' and پاسخ is its object. The conjunction که introduces the content clause 'that ...' and شبگیر شاه is a nominal phrase 'the night-visitor king' (شبگیر used as an epithet for the king).
segments.s612-g110: بیامد is the 3rd-person past 'he came'. سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)'. دشت نخچیرگاه is a compound noun phrase 'the plain (دشت) of the hunting-ground (نخچیرگاه)' — in poetry the ezāfe linking them is often omitted but the second word modifies the first.
segments.s612-g111: بلنگید is a past verb (poetic/archaic form related to لنگیدن) used figuratively here; in context it conveys a drooping/sagging or faltering action. در زیر من is a locative phrase 'under me' (در + زیر + pronoun من), and بارگی is 'a leaf/foliage'. The whole clause therefore describes a leaf or foliage drooping beneath the speaker.
segments.s612-g112: ازو is the archaic contracted prepositional form 'from him/it' (از + او). بازگشتم is first-person past 'I returned/turned back'. به بیچارگی is a prepositional phrase 'into/with misery' indicating the speaker's wretched state — 'I turned away from him in misery.'
segments.s612-g233: بروبر is an adverb meaning 'directly / face-to-face' and introduces the following clause; ازان گونه (literally 'from that manner') is a fixed phrase meaning 'in that way', and شد مبتلا is the compound predicate 'became afflicted' where شد is the past of شدن (become) and مبتلا is a predicative adjective/noun 'afflicted/stricken'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect but the core is: (adverb) + (manner phrase) + became + predicate.
segments.s612-g234: که گفتی here is a parenthetical 'as you said' (که + second-person past گفتی), introducing the reported remark; دلش گشت is 'his heart turned / became' where ش is the possessive clitic 'his'; گنج بلا is a genitival nominal phrase 'treasure of affliction' (گنج 'treasure' modified by بلا 'affliction'). گشت is the verbal 'became/turned' linking subject to the nominal predicate.
segments.s612-g235: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; در پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'in his presence' (در + پیش + pronoun او); مست شد is 'became drunk' (مست predicate with شد auxiliary); ماهیار is the person (name/epithet) and appears after the verb by poetic inversion, so the clause reads 'When, in his presence, Mahyār became intoxicated...'.
segments.s612-g236: چنین گفت 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); با میزبان شهریار is the prepositional phrase 'to/with the king's host' where با marks the addressee/companion and میزبان شهریار is a genitive construction 'the host of the king'. The clause is a speech introduction: 'he said thus to the king's host.'
segments.s612-g237: که here introduces reported speech/command ('that' / 'as one says'); the core command is دختر به من ده — 'give the daughter to me' (ده is the imperative of دادن, implied in the clause); به آیین و دین is an adverbial phrase 'in accordance with rite and religion' (= 'properly, according to custom and law'). Word order is imperative + indirect object + manner phrase.
segments.s612-g238: چو خواهی is conditional 'if you wish' (چو = 'if' here, خواهی = 'you want/wish'); که introduces the subordinate result/clause and یابی is the present-subjunctive/optative 'you may find/obtain' (expressing potential/hope). به داد آفرین is an adverbial phrase: به + داد (here 'justice' or a worthy act) + آفرین (praiseworthy/commendation), functioning to qualify the manner/result ('may you obtain it with laudable justice').
segments.s612-g239: چنین گفت 'thus he said' is the speech marker; با آرزو 'with longing' indicates the speaker's manner or feeling while speaking (با + noun آرزو), and ماهیار is the speaker's name placed after the phrase for emphasis (poetic word order).
segments.s612-g240: کزین = 'from this' (ک + زین) introduces the source/object; شیردل 'lion‑hearted' is the person described; چند is the interrogative 'how much'; خواهی is present 2nd‑person 'will you/would you like to' expressing intent; نثار is a noun/verb 'offering / to bestow' — together they form the question 'How much will you bestow from this lion‑hearted one?' with خواهی governing the notion of intending to give and نثار naming the gift/offer.
segments.s612-g161: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. شد here is a past/preterite-like perfective 'became/was' (literally 'became washed'); دست شسته is a nominative noun + participle phrase 'hand washed' = 'his hands having been washed'. می‌و جام is an enumerated object (wine/cup) placed before the past 3rd-person verb خواست 'he wanted'. The verb خواست is simple past 3rd-sg.
segments.s612-g162: به is the preposition 'for/to'. می‌رامش is a verbal noun/infinitival sense 'to soothe (with wine)' and is governed by به. The coordination و joins parallel desiderata (نام و آرام = 'renown and calm'); خواست is repeated (past 3rd-sg) and applies to both coordinated phrases, a poetic parallelism.
segments.s612-g163: کنیزک is the subject 'the maidservant'. بیاورد is a past-tense narrative verb 'brought' (3rd-sg). جامی is an indefinite object 'a cup' (the -ی suffix), and نبید names the content 'nebīd' (wine/juice). The verb precedes its object here as is common in narrative lines.
segments.s612-g164: This segment is an asyndetic/asyntactic list of items brought: می‌سرخ (reddish wine) and جام (cup) and گل (flower/rose) and شنبلید (a plant, fenugreek/hyacinth-like). The conjunction و links the nouns; می‌سرخ functions adjectivally/nominally to describe the wine.
segments.s612-g165: بیازید is an older/poetic verb meaning roughly 'opened/gaped/put to mouth' or 'reached forth'; it governs the following subject دهقان 'the peasant'. به جام is the directional phrase 'to the cup', and از نخست is an adverbial 'from the first / at first' modifying the following verb بخورد.
segments.s612-g166: بخورد is past 3rd-sg of خوردن used here as 'he drank/took a sip'. The conjunction و links the next clause: به مشک و گلابش بشست means 'he washed with musk and his rosewater' — به marks the instrument/means and گلابش carries the possessive suffix -ش 'his'. بشست is past 3rd-sg 'he washed'.
segments.s612-g167: به بهرام داد is 'gave to Bahram' (به marks the indirect object; داد is past 3rd-sg 'gave'). آن دلارای جام is the definite direct object 'that dear/charming cup' (آن + adjective دلارای + noun جام) following the verb in poetic order.
segments.s612-g168: بدو گفت is the common phrasing 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said' in narrative). The clause میخواره را چیست نام is an inverted interrogative: میخواره 'the wine‑drinker' marked by the object marker را, followed by the copula‑question construction چیست ('is what') and the noun نام 'name' — literally 'the wine‑drinker, what is (his) name?'.
segments.s612-g425: This is a verbless nominal clause common in Persian poetry: preposition + noun phrase 'به بالای تو بر زمین' (= 'at/above your height on the earth') is placed before the existential predicate 'نیست' ('is not'). Word order is topical: 'به بالای تو' (location/standard) + 'بر زمین' (ground) + 'شاه' (subject/thing expected) + 'نیست' (negation of existence).
segments.s612-g426: Same verbless construction repeated in parallel: 'به دیدار تو بر فلک' (locative/ comparative frame: 'in view of you / before your sight in the sky') + 'ماه' (the expected noun) + 'نیست' (is not). 'دیدار' here functions as 'sight/seeing' rather than a substantive visit. The line uses parallelism to compare earthly kings and the moon to the addressee.
segments.s612-g427: 'سپاهی که بیند سپاه ترا' is a relative clause: 'سپاهی' (an army/force) + relative particle 'که' ('that/who') + verb 'بیند' (present subjunctive/indicative 'sees'). 'سپاه' + enclitic 'ت' (here written apart) + object marker 'را' together mark 'your host' ('سپاهِ تو را'). The clause means 'an army which sees your army'.
segments.s612-g428: This continues the action of the relative clause: 'به جنگ اندر آوردگاه ترا' — 'به جنگ' ('to/into war') + adverbial 'اندر' ('in/into') + 'آوردگاه' ('battlefield') followed by the enclitic object 'ت' + object marker 'را' (together 'you' as object). Verb 'آورد' 'to bring' is nominalized here in the compound noun 'آوردگاه' and the sense is idiomatic: they bring (their) forces into the battlefield to confront you. The structure places the goal ('به جنگ اندر آوردگاه') before the object ('ترا'), typical of Persian poetic ordering.
segments.s612-g429: Phrase structure: 'بدرد دل و مغزشان از نهیب' uses 'بدرد' in its causative/pain sense ('afflicts / does harm to') followed by the two nouns 'دل و مغز' ('heart and brain') with the possessive enclitic 'شان' ('their'). 'از نهیب' ('from the shout') gives the cause. So: 'their hearts and brains are affected by the loud cry.'
segments.s612-g430: The predicate 'ندانند' ('they do not know') follows the affected nouns and takes a contrastive object phrase 'بلندی ... از نشیب' ('the high ... from the low'). Word order is inverted for poetic effect: the quality 'بلندی' (height/high) comes before the verb; 'از نشیب' marks the thing from which it is distinguished. Note: the dictionary senses provided for 'بلندی' do not match the lexical use here (height), so sense index is not available.
segments.s612-g431: 'هم‌انگه چو' is a temporal marker: 'هم‌انگه' ('at that moment') + 'چو' used as 'when'. 'از باده خرم شدند' is a causative/result clause: 'از باده' ('from wine') + 'خرم شدند' ('they became merry/joyful'); the verb is past plural 'شدند' (became). The clause sets the time and cause for what follows.
segments.s612-g432: 'ز خردک به جام دمادم شدند' literally 'from the small cup to the cup they were continually' — 'ز' = 'from', 'خردک' = 'small measure/small cup' (here small drinking measure), 'به جام' = 'to the goblet/cup', 'دمادم' = adverb 'constantly/again and again', and 'شدند' is the past plural 'they became/they were' used idiomatically to mean they kept on doing the drinking action. The phrase describes continuous drinking from small to larger cups.
segments.s612-g65: بخواهد is the 3rd-person present/future-form of خواستن used here to mean ‘he will/wishes to’ (a volitional/future idea). همان is a demonstrative ‘that/the very’, modifying دخترش (‘his daughter’). از پدر is a source phrase ‘from (her) father’ indicating from whom she will be taken.
segments.s612-g66: نهد is the 3rd-person verb (from نهاد) ‘places/puts’ (here future/result of the prior desire). بی‌گمان is an adverb ‘undoubtedly/without doubt’ modifying the clause. بر سرش is a prepositional phrase ‘upon her head’ where -ش is the enclitic possesive ‘her/his’. تاج زر is a noun + modifier ‘a crown of gold’ (تاج = crown, زر = gold).
segments.s612-g67: نیابد is the negative compound (ن + یابد) meaning ‘does not / will not find’ (here general/habitual negative). همی is the older continuous/iterative particle marking ongoing or habitual action (‘still/continually’). سیری is an abstract noun ‘satisfaction, satiety’. از خفت و خیز is a source/phrase ‘from sleep and rising’ — two nouns juxtaposed to mean ‘sleeping and waking’ or ‘sleep and getting up’.
segments.s612-g68: شب تیره is a noun + adjective ‘the dark night’. ز (here zv/زو) is the poetic form of از + او ‘from him’. جفت گیرد is a verb phrase: جفت ‘mate/companion’ as object and گیرد (گیرد = ‘seizes/takes’) the 3rd-person verb. گریز ‘flight/escape’ follows to indicate that after seizing the companion the night ‘takes flight’ — a compact poetic coordinate meaning ‘seizes his mate and flees’.
segments.s612-g69: شبستان is a classical word for ‘bedchamber/inner chamber’. مر is the enclitic/dative form ‘to me’ placed after the noun (common in older Persian). او را here marks the possessor (‘his’) attached after the possessed noun in classical order (literally ‘his bedchamber’ with را as a marker). فزون از صد است is the predicate ‘is more than a hundred’ (فزون از = ‘more than’, followed by the numeral صد; است is the copula).
segments.s612-g70: شهنشاه is ‘the emperor/king’. زین‌سان is an adverbial phrase ‘thus/so’. که introduces the subordinate clause (‘that/so that’), and باشد is the present/subjunctive copular form ‘may be / would be’ used here in a classical periphrastic sense; بد است is the judgement ‘is bad’. Together: ‘For the king to be so would be bad.’
segments.s612-g71: کنون is the temporal adverb ‘now’. نه‌صد و سی is the compound numeral ‘nine hundred and thirty’ (classical written compounding). زن is ‘woman/wife’ (here plural by context). از مهتران means ‘from among the chiefs/great ones’ (مهتران = the important/elder/great ones). The phrase gives the group: ‘now nine hundred thirty women of the great/chief ones.’
segments.s612-g72: همه is ‘all/everyone’ as the subject. بر سران is the locative phrase ‘upon (their) heads’ (سران is the plural of سر = heads). افسر here means a crown/diadem/ornamental helmet placed on the head (classical usage), and از گوهران ‘of gems’ specifies the material/embellishment. Together: ‘all (have) on their heads diadems of gems.’
segments.s612-g441: بیامد is a past tense verb (archaic/poetic form ‘came’); here it stands before its subject, a common poetic word order (verb first). شهنشاه is the subject ('the Shahanshah, the emperor'). با + روزبه is a prepositional phrase 'with Rouzbeh' modifying the verb (he arrived accompanied by Rouzbeh).
segments.s612-g442: گشاده‌دل و شاد are coordinated adjectival predicates describing the Shahanshah ('open‑/warm‑hearted and happy'); adjectives commonly follow the noun/subject in poetic Persian. از is the source‑preposition 'from'; ایوان مه is a noun + epithet 'the iwan/portico of the moon' (مه here = 'moon', used poetically to mean 'moonlit' or 'of the moon').
segments.s612-g443: همی‌راند is an epic/imperfective past ('kept driving/rode along'); the prefix همی marks continuous or iterative action in older/poetic Persian. گویان is a participial/gerundial adverb ('saying'/'chanting') indicating manner ('while saying'). به is directional 'toward'; مشکوی is an adjective 'musk‑scented'. خوی + ش together form خویش ('his own'): here the lexical piece خوی is split from the enclitic ش, so the two combine to mean 'his'.
segments.s612-g444: به سوی is a compound directional phrase 'toward(s)'. بتان is the plural noun (here poetically likely 'beloveds/beautiful ones' rather than strictly idols). سمن‑بوی is an adjective after the noun ('jasmine‑scented'), and again خوی + ش (= خویش) function as the possessive 'his own' attached to that phrase.
segments.s612-g17: بدان here is the poetic contraction of به + آن meaning 'to that / into that' (prepositional phrase) and stands before the rest of the clause for emphasis. مرغزار 'meadow' is the place-object; اندرون is an adverb 'inside/within' and راند is the past tense verb 'drove/expelled' (3rd person). شاه appears after the verb as the subject by poetic inversion (V–S order instead of the more neutral S–V), so the segment reads 'the king drove (into) that meadow'.
segments.s612-g18: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from (among) the army' that supplies source/agent for the action. هرانکس که introduces a restrictive relative 'whoever / anyone who…' and بد + نیک‌خواه is a compound describing disposition: بد qualifies نیک‌خواه to mean 'ill-/bad‑wisher' or 'one of bad intent.' The whole segment therefore specifies 'anyone from the army who was ill-disposed.'
segments.s612-g19: یکی بیشه دیدند is 'they saw a certain thicket' — یکی is an indefinitive 'a certain/one', بیشه 'thicket/grove' is the place seen, and دیدند is the past plural 'they saw'. پر گوسفند is a postpositive description meaning 'full of sheep' (پر here = 'full (of)') modifying بیشه. The verb appears after the subject phrase as common in narrative.
segments.s612-g20: شبانان گریزان is a nominal phrase 'the shepherds, fleeing' where گریزان is a participial/adjectival form 'fleeing/avoiding' used predicatively. ز بیم گزند is an ablative/cause phrase 'from fear of harm' (ز = 'from/because of', بیم = 'fear', گزند = 'harm'), explaining why the shepherds were fleeing.
segments.s612-g21: یکی سرشبان دید بهرام را = 'a certain head-shepherd saw Bahram.' یکی is 'a certain' (introducing an individual), سرشبان is the subject 'head-shepherd', دید is the past tense 'saw', and بهرام را is a definite object with the object marker را (indicating a specific person). The order (subject — verb — object with را) is a poetic/narrative arrangement.
segments.s612-g22: بر او دوید = 'ran toward him': بر + (او) here means 'toward/to' and دوید is past 'ran/hastened'. از پی نام را is an adverbial phrase 'after (calling) his name / in pursuit of (his) name' where از پی = 'after/following' and نام را (name + object marker) indicates the thing being called or pursued; the whole segment describes the shepherd running up to Bahram, apparently calling his name.
segments.s612-g23: بدو گفت بهرام = 'to him Bahram said' (بدو = 'to him/unto it' placed before the verb). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here reported speech) and بهرام is the speaker (subject) following the verb — a common word order in poetry. کاین گوسفند begins a relative/demonstrative 'this/that sheep' that the reported speech will describe.
segments.s612-g24: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; آرد is the present or habitual form 'brings' (here used by Ferdowsi in the sense 'brings/causes'). بدین is the poetic contraction of به + این 'to this', presented as ب + دین in the segmentation, and جای ناسودمند is the predicate 'a place unprofitable' (ناسودمند = 'unprofitable, fruitless'). Grammatically the clause says 'which brings to this place no benefit' describing the sheep.
segments.s612-g257: اگر is the conditional particle 'if'. بشمری is a subjunctive/imperative-form of شمردن 'to count' used here after اگر, meaning 'if you were to count' or 'if you count'. گوهر ماهیار is the object phrase 'the jewels (گوهر) of/like Mahyar' where ماهیار is an epithet ('moon‑like' or a name) modifying گوهر; word order is verb (بشمری) followed by its object in poetic inversion.
segments.s612-g258: فزون آید is a predicate meaning 'will be more / will exceed' (فزون 'more' + آید 'comes/appears'). از introduces the standard comparative 'than', followed by بدره 'full moon' used metaphorically for beauty; شهریار at the end addresses the ruler ('O king') or may qualify بدره as 'the king's (object)', depending on vocative sense. The line thus compares quantity/beauty to the moon.
segments.s612-g259: گر is a conditional 'if'. او را marks 'him' as the object/topic. همی is an archaic emphatic/progressive particle here ('indeed/just' or continuous emphasis) attached to the following predicate. بایدت is the construction 'it is necessary for you / you ought' (باید with clitic -ت), and جام‌گیر is the predicate noun 'cup‑bearer' (one who holds the cup). Together the clause says 'if you regard him as a cupbearer / if he is to be your cupbearer.'
segments.s612-g260: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'. سرسری is an adverb meaning 'superficially/without care' modifying the verb. امشب is the temporal adverb 'tonight'. آرام‌گیر functions as an imperative/hortative 'make (him) calm / give (him) rest' (lit. 'take/bring calm'). The sequence commands: 'Do not be cursory; tonight, soothe/let him rest.'
segments.s612-g261: به مستی بزرگان is a prepositional phrase 'in the drunkenness/ecstasy of the great'. نبستند is the past‑tense negative 'they did not bind' (3rd person plural of بستن). بند is the direct object 'fetter/bond'. The clause says literally that no fetters were bound upon (or restraint imposed on) the great while they were intoxicated.
segments.s612-g262: به ویژه functions as the compound adverb 'especially'. کسی کو is an older-style relative phrase meaning 'someone who' (کو here serving as a relative particle). بود is the past copula 'was', and ارجمند is the predicate adjective 'esteemed/precious'. The clause restricts the prior statement particularly to 'anyone who was esteemed.'
segments.s612-g263: بمان is the imperative/hortative 'remain/stay' addressed to the addressee. تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until'. برآرد is a 3rd‑person singular verb 'brings forth' or 'causes to appear'. سپهر آفتاب is a poetic word order for 'the heaven/sky of the sun' meaning 'the sky brings the sun' or 'sunrise'. Together: 'Stay until the sky brings forth the sun.'
segments.s612-g264: سر نامداران is a nominal phrase 'the head(s) of the famed/illustrious ones' (سر used collectively for leaders). برآید is 'will arise/appear' (3rd‑person singular used with the collective). ز is the preposition 'from', and خواب is 'sleep'. The clause says that the leaders/renowned will rise from sleep (i.e., awake).
segments.s612-g281: بدو گفت is a short clause meaning 'he said to him' with the indirect-object prepositional phrase (بدو, 'to him') placed before the past verb گفت. کاکنون is an archaic fused form (= که اکنون) meaning 'now'; تو is the 2nd-person subject. جفت ویی is a predicative nominal construction with the copula omitted: جفت 'mate/pair' is the predicate and ویی (archaic third‑person pronoun/possessive) marks whose mate it is, so the phrase reads 'you are his mate.'
segments.s612-g282: چنان دان is an imperative-like instruction: چنان 'thus/so' + دان 'know/consider' (here used as 'know/consider it so'). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/which'; اندر 'in/within' modifies نهفت ('hidden', here a past participle or adjectival form), and ویی again is the archaic third-person pronoun referring back to the person/thing concealed. The clause therefore means 'know it so — that which he has hidden within.'
segments.s612-g283: بدو داد places the indirect object (بدو 'to him') before the past verb داد 'gave'. The coordinating و links to the next clause. In بهرام گورش بخواست the noun بهرام is the subject and گورش ('his grave/tomb' — گور + possessive ش) is the object; بخواست is past tense 'sought/desired/asked for' (context determines whether 'asked for' or 'ordered').
segments.s612-g284: چو ... شد is a temporal subordinate clause: چو 'when' + شب روز شد 'night became day' (inversion is poetic). The main clause is کار او گشت راست where کار 'the task/work' is the subject, او 'his' marks possessor, گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned', and راست 'right/true' is the resulting predicate — 'his task became right' i.e. was rightly accomplished.
segments.s612-g285: سوی حجره خویش رفت is a normal motion clause: سوی حجره خویش 'toward his chamber' (سوی + noun + خویش 'his own') precedes the past verb رفت 'went'. آرزوی is a noun 'desire/wish' used adverbially here to indicate purpose ('with the desire of' = 'wishing to'), so the whole segment reads 'he went toward his chamber wishing (to compose).'
segments.s612-g286: سرایش is the noun 'composition/singing' and here completes the idea 'آرزوی سرایش' = 'a desire to compose/sing.' همه خفته is a reduced predication 'all (were) asleep.' بد چار سوی is an archaic poetic locution for 'به چهار سو' (to the four directions) meaning 'in every direction/around'; thus 'all were asleep on all sides.'
segments.s612-g287: بیامد is the past verb 'came' and here begins the clause; به جای دگر is a prepositional phrase 'to another place' (به جای = to the place of; دگر = other). ماهیار is the proper name (the subject), placed after the verb in the typical poetic/older Persian word order.
segments.s612-g288: همی is a progressive/adverbial particle ('kept on/continually'); ساخت is past 'made/did' and here indicates he 'made' or 'prepared' something. کار is the object 'work/affair/preparations'. گشسپ سوار is a name plus an appositive/epithet — 'Goshasp the rider/horseman' — so the genitive relationship is implied (either 'work for Goshasp the rider' or 'the work of Goshasp the rider') depending on context.
segments.s612-g393: ز + noun (نادانی) is a prepositional phrase meaning 'because of/from ignorance.' آمد is a simple past verb 'came/occurred' placed before the predicate for poetic inversion. گنهکاریم is a present/stative predicate with the 1st-person-plural ending -یم meaning 'we are sinful' or 'we became sinful,' so the whole clause reads literally 'From ignorance came our being sinful.'
