segments.s612-g394: گمانم is 'I suppose/I suspect' with the enclitic -م 'I'; که introduces a subordinate clause 'that.' دیوانه is the predicate adjective 'mad.' پنداریم here functions idiomatically as 'are thought/considered' (an older/poetic usage where the verb expresses being regarded), so 'گمانم که دیوانه پنداریم' = 'I suppose that we are regarded as mad.'
segments.s612-g395: سزد‌گر is a conditional/agentive expression 'if it is fitting' or 'one who deems fit' introducing a conditional clause. ببخشی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of بخشیدن 'to forgive' (here 'you forgive' / 'you may forgive'). گناه مرا is the object 'my sin' with the possessive clitic and the object marker; the object marker (را) is separated in the word list.
segments.s612-g396: درفشان is an adjectival/epithetic word 'shining, resplendent' describing the effect to be caused; کنی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن 'to make' (so 'you would make'). روز و ماه is a common idiom 'days and months' (= time/days), and مرا (possessive + object marker) indicates 'my days and months' as the thing to be made shining.
segments.s612-g397: منم is emphatic 'I am.' بر + درت is a prepositional phrase 'upon/at your door' with the possessive clitic -ت attached to در. بنده is 'servant' and بی‌خرد is an adjective 'foolish/unwise' modifying بنده; word order here is typical poetic/colloquial noun + adjective.
segments.s612-g398: شهنشاهم is 'my shahanshah' (possessive suffix -م). نشمرد is the negative past/present 3rd-person form 'did not count/does not count' (ن- prefixed to شمرد). از بخردان is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of the wise' used idiomatically as 'among the wise' — the clause means 'my shahanshah does not reckon me among the wise.'
segments.s612-g399: چنین داد پاسخ is 'gave such an answer' (چنین = 'thus/so', داد = 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). که introduces the reported clause. از مرد مست is a prepositional phrase 'from a drunken man' with مست qualifying مرد.
segments.s612-g400: خردمند ('a wise man') is the subject. چیزی is the indefinite object 'anything' (often placed before the verb for emphasis). نگیرد is a present/subjunctive 3rd-person negative of گرفتن 'to take' used here as a general maxim ('does not/should not take'). به دست is an adverbial phrase 'by hand' (manner). The clause means 'a wise man does not take anything by hand (from a drunken man).'

sentences.s612-s153: They demanded the army from the rebellious chiefs, and took that whip as a token.
sentences.s612-s154: The army formed an assembly at the court, remaining at the king's gate.
sentences.s612-s155: Whoever recognized the newcomer returned, and they brought prayer before him.
sentences.s612-s156: When the doorkeeper saw those warriors — many girded and wearing padded jackets.
sentences.s612-s45: A young man may become humpbacked on his right side — from the deeds of women come many kinds of calamities.
sentences.s612-s46: If intercourse/mixing happens more than once a month, there will be bleeding.
sentences.s612-s47: This very burden, for the sake of the child, must be borne by the wise young man.
sentences.s612-s48: If you increase it, the diminution increases; from bodily weakness a man becomes bloodless.
sentences.s612-s25: The head‑groom said to him, “Go from here; you will be given fresh (things) before anything new comes to you.”
sentences.s612-s26: A sound from that place reached the city, near the palace of Bahram Shah.
sentences.s612-s27: When the sky donned black silk, that man came to the feast in full pomp.
sentences.s612-s28: If you delay a little, sweet sounds and the song of the harp will come to your ear.
sentences.s612-s61: When the king entered, he beheld such a place; he saw worshippers standing in every spot.
sentences.s612-s62: He said thus: 'O just one, God, guide your servant into your goodness.'
sentences.s612-s63: May it not be, except by justice, the rule of mine; may greed and stubborn defiance not be in my religion.
sentences.s612-s64: May all my work and conduct be justice; may the hearts of my subjects be glad toward us.
sentences.s612-s21: Tell me who it was that killed these beasts — may the God of the world be his support.
sentences.s612-s22: Bahram said to him, 'These two lions were slain by the arrow of a brave man.'
sentences.s612-s23: When he had slain the war‑lions, he rode off — a proud horseman with seven companions.
sentences.s612-s24: Where is the jeweler's portico? Make it appear — show the way and do not hide it from us.
sentences.s612-s145: He told the attendant, “Close the doors; send one to gallop after the sheep.”
sentences.s612-s146: It must not be that they set a table without a lamb; the lamb too must be properly reared and pure.
sentences.s612-s147: When the froth and the ice grow awake, stand before Goshasp the horseman.
sentences.s612-s148: Prepare a cup of camphor with rosewater; do it so that the sleeping-place may be fragrant.
sentences.s612-s13: To him the head-groom said, 'O king, I have come from the world to this meadow.'
sentences.s612-s14: These very sheep, the jeweler’s, I brought into the plain yesterday from the mountain.
sentences.s612-s15: May the wealthy lord ever turn this sheep away from the harm of reproach.
sentences.s612-s16: It is a kharvar (a large measure) of famed gems; indeed gold and silver and also ornaments.
sentences.s612-s189: The king said to him, “Where did you have this? You made me drunk and then left me.”
sentences.s612-s190: That very poem and harp are enough for us; the offerings intended for women are for someone else.
sentences.s612-s191: Bring what you spoke of from the hunting-ground — (that) about battle, the spear's tip, and the king's wound.
sentences.s612-s192: Thereupon the jeweler said to him, 'Where has he gone who last night made us drunk?'
sentences.s612-s121: Watch him until he pleases you; it will be useful for your ease.
sentences.s612-s122: Thus Arzuy spoke to Mahyar: 'Who are you, O Bab — noble and good‑natured?'
sentences.s612-s123: If you would give me to anyone, my companion is Goshasp the rider — and that's enough.
sentences.s612-s124: You might say he would stay with Bahram — for he is dear as life, and to sit with him even a moment...

sentences.s612-s166: A whip wrought with gold, with gems woven in every place.
sentences.s612-s167: He hung it before our gate, on that side which is our passage.
sentences.s612-s168: When the gatekeeper heard the speech, the old alert man immediately turned.
sentences.s612-s73: They also called the worshipper to the feast, and set up another place (for him).
sentences.s612-s74: They placed that same host in a low seat, and he sat down near the king.
sentences.s612-s75: Having made an apology, then the host said to Bahram, 'O marzban, speak!'
sentences.s612-s76: I am among the guests in this khan; may my body and soul be sacrificed for you.
sentences.s612-s49: They went, saying, to the king's iwan; one of them said, 'The sun has lost its way.'
sentences.s612-s50: Into the dark night went Bahram Gūr; a man worshipping one person for the sake of a steed.
